AI.result: The study demonstrates that 3D FaceShell significantly increases attribute injection and mismatch rates in VLMs while maintaining perceptual similarity and identity consistency.
AI.conclusion: 3D FaceShell proves effective in mitigating VLM-based privacy risks for 3D face avatars, enabling semantic control without degrading visual or identity-related integrity.

title: Operator-Aware Mixed-Precision Tolerance Calibration for Tensor Kernels
comment: 8 pages, 1 figure, LNCS format. Companion paper: arXiv:2606.20128 (P1). Additional companions (P3, P4) to follow on arXiv this week; IDs will be added in a v2 replace
summary:
Most tensor-kernel correctness tests go through a fixed-shape all close-style check with hand-picked absolute and relative tolerances. The thresholds are copied across the corpus and rarely revisited. We mine the element-wise error distribution of every test case from accumulated cloud GPU runs across the 26-entry gpuemu corpus and 2 dtypes (8,076 result rows). We then ask one empirical question: what absolute tolerance would the kernel itself, observed under its correct implementation, justify?
  The answer is much tighter than the current hand-picked atol. The largest tightening is attention_triton fp16 at $2{,}184\times$. Restricted to the seven LLM-style buggy variants for which the corpus ships a paired correct counterpart, calibrated per-(op, dtype) tolerances raise bug-detection recall from 73.2% (1,805 of 2,467) to 82.4% (2,034 of 2,467), an absolute gain of 9.3 percentage points (+229 new detections). The control false-positive count rises from 0 to 20 out of 1,882 correct-control cases (+1.1 percentage points).

AI.tldr: This study improves tensor-kernel correctness tests by analyzing error distributions from actual runs, finding justifiable absolute tolerances much tighter than current ones, significantly raising bug-detection recall.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address suboptimal bug-detection in tensor-kernel correctness tests caused by hand-picked and outdated tolerance thresholds.
AI.method: They analyze the element-wise error distributions of kernels using empirical data from GPU runs across a 26-entry corpus for two data types, deriving tailored absolute tolerances.
AI.result: The study significantly tightens absolute tolerances, achieving substantial improvements in bug-detection recall from 73.2% to 82.4% (+9.3 percentage points) while introducing a small rise in false positives from 0 to 20 (+1.1 percentage points).
AI.conclusion: Tailoring absolute tolerances empirically improves the accuracy of correctness tests, enabling better bug detection with minimal cost in false positives.

title: Provably Good Prim-Dijkstra Revisited: New Theory and a Practical Algorithm for a Classical VLSI Routing Problem with LLMs
comment: 23 pages, 6 figures. Code and reproducibility materials: https://github.com/CODA-Team/hp-rcrst
summary: Large language models may make precise but dormant algorithmic problems practical to revisit, and may expose new paths toward fundamental ones. We demonstrate this possibility through Prim-Dijkstra routing, a classic VLSI problem whose terminal-only Manhattan complexity remained open despite decades of practical work. We prove weak NP-completeness, derive a continuous cost-radius tradeoff with a balanced (2,2) guarantee, and build HP-RCRST, a height-partition-based multi-mode solver. On 28 development instances, its stronger modes Pareto-dominate the published-method union on 23 and tie on five. The case shows how conflicting conjectures, counterexamples, formal checks, and implementation can reopen neglected questions. Code and reproducibility materials are available at https://github.com/CODA-Team/hp-rcrst.

AI.tldr: The study addresses Prim-Dijkstra routing, proving weak NP-completeness, deriving a cost-radius tradeoff, and introducing HP-RCRST solver that improves on previously published methods.
AI.motivation: To revisit and solve a dormant algorithmic problem in VLSI, specifically the complexity of terminal-only Manhattan routing, aided by advancements in large language models.
AI.method: The paper proves weak NP-completeness, establishes a balancing guarantee in cost-radius tradeoff (2,2), and develops HP-RCRST, a height-partition-based multi-mode solver.
AI.result: HP-RCRST outperformed previously published methods in 23 out of 28 instances and tied in the remaining 5, demonstrating its efficacy in solving the routing problem.
AI.conclusion: Algorithmic problems deemed impractical can be revisited with new tools like language models. The proposed solver exhibits stronger performance, reopening fundamental algorithmic questions.

title: Organization of computation in reservoir computing
summary: Reservoir computing exploits nonlinear dynamical systems to encode temporal inputs into high-dimensional state space representations. Although reservoir performance is often characterized through memory, nonlinearity, and their tradeoff, such aggregate measures do not reveal how task-relevant information is organized within the state space. Here, we introduce an eigen-spectral decomposition framework linking the degree-wise information processing capacity to the corresponding state space modes. As a result, we are able to quantify the degree-wise representation energy, and show that in some cases, substantial amounts of information processing capacity may reside in low-energy modes that are vulnerable to experimental noise. These results suggest that useful reservoir computation depends not only on dimensionality expansion, but also on the geometric organization of task-relevant information, with direct implications on physical reservoir computers.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an eigen-spectral framework to analyze the state space in reservoir computing, focusing on the organization of task-relevant information instead of aggregate measures of memory and nonlinearity.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to move beyond the aggregate measures of memory and nonlinearity in reservoir computing and provide insights into how task-relevant information is organized within the state space.
AI.method: The authors propose an eigen-spectral decomposition framework to analyze the degree-wise information processing capacity and state space modes of reservoir computing systems.
AI.result: The study reveals that significant information processing capacity can exist in low-energy modes that are sensitive to experimental noise. This introduces new insights into the geometric organization of task-relevant information.
AI.conclusion: The findings emphasize the importance of not just expanding dimensionality but also ensuring the effective geometric organization of task-relevant information in reservoir computing, which has practical implications for the design of physical reservoir computers.

title: ETAS: An Effect-Typed Language for Agent Systems
summary:
ETAS is a programming language for agent systems that treats model-backed agents, tool calls, prompts, typed memory, human approvals, policies, and execution traces as semantic program elements rather than library conventions. It separates deterministic computation from agentic nondeterminism and externally visible actions while preserving a direct programming style.
  We present the core design of ETAS. Its static semantics assigns ordinary types through spec conformance and tracks each computation with two behavioral indices: an escaping effect row and a persistent abstraction of the typed action trace it may request. Specs form a terminating compile-time constraint calculus: type specs provide evidence for polymorphism and resource facts, callable specs constrain function and stage shapes, and trace specs express allow, deny, and temporal constraints. Typing checks requested traces against compiled monitors and emits residual obligations when dynamic resources preclude a complete static proof. The dynamic semantics distinguish requested, handled, denied, and committed events; handlers interpret typed actions without making their requests invisible to authorization or audit.
  We formalize a core calculus and state preservation, progress, type/effect soundness, handler trace-transparency, and policy safety. We also implement ETAS in Rust with a command-line interface, typed HIR checks, effect and policy diagnostics, handler checks, and trace-aware execution hooks. ETAS provides a programming-language foundation for reasoning about authorization, nondeterminism, recovery, and audit evidence before and during agent execution.

AI.tldr: ETAS is a programming language designed for agent systems that incorporates model-backed agents and other semantic program elements, enabling structured computation, authorization, and trace monitoring.
AI.motivation: To create a programming language foundation that treats agent-specific elements as semantic constructs to improve authorization, nondeterminism, recovery, and audit processes before and during execution.
AI.method: ETAS involves core design principles including a static semantics system to assign types and track computations with behavioral indices, alongside a dynamic semantics framework for event handling and authorization visibility. A Rust implementation with CLI and diagnostic tools supports its practical integration.
AI.result: The paper formalizes key theoretical properties like type/effect soundness and policy safety, and demonstrates ETAS with practical implementation features such as HIR checks and trace-aware execution hooks.
AI.conclusion: ETAS advances programming paradigms by treating agent-specific behaviors as primary elements, enabling structured reasoning around constraints, authorization, and execution evidence for agent systems.

title: Technical Report: AI-Assisted Gated DeltaNet Optimization on NVIDIA Blackwell
summary: AI-assisted GPU programming is often framed as a kernel-generation loop: ask a model to produce faster CUDA code, benchmark the result, and repeat. This case study argues that contest-grade optimization involves more than improving the kernel body. We examine the Agent-Assisted submission by our team, MSInfer, to the MLSys 2026 FlashInfer Contest. The submission optimized Gated DeltaNet decode and prefill on NVIDIA B200/Blackwell and achieved an official $1.58\times$ speedup, with approximate average latencies of $9.315\,μ\mathrm{s}$ for decode and $239.48\,μ\mathrm{s}$ for prefill. Our experience shows that even effective local kernel improvements can plateau when a workload requires structural reformulation and evaluator-aligned measurement. We therefore characterize AI-assisted kernel optimization as an end-to-end systems problem that encompasses algorithm design, workload specialization, measurement tooling, build and evaluation surfaces, evaluator alignment, and human interpretation.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates AI-assisted GPU programming optimization, achieving a $1.58x$ speedup in a contest, and concludes that optimization requires end-to-end systems restructuring, not just kernel improvement.
AI.motivation: To explore and demonstrate that high-grade performance optimization in GPU programming extends beyond kernel improvement to involve holistic systems-level reformulation.
AI.method: Analyzed their team's submission to MLSys 2026 FlashInfer Contest for Gated DeltaNet decode and prefill on NVIDIA B200/Blackwell systems, focusing on both kernel optimization and broader structural reformulation.
AI.result: Achieved $1.58x$ speedup with approximate latencies of $9.315 μs$ for decode and $239.48 μs$ for prefill in the competition.
AI.conclusion: Effective GPU optimization requires addressing broader systems challenges like evaluator alignment, measurement tuning, and workload structuring, alongside local kernel improvements.

title: Deterministic Replay for AI Agent Systems
summary:
AI agent systems that couple large language models (LLMs) with external tools and APIs are inherently non-deterministic: LLM sampling variance, external API state, CDN infrastructure headers, and execution-environment noise collectively prevent any prior agent run from being faithfully re-executed. Existing observability platforms capture execution logs but cannot reproduce a run in isolation. We present agrepl, a developer-first CLI framework for deterministic replay of agent executions. agrepl intercepts all external interactions at the transport layer via a man-in-the-middle (MITM) proxy, serialises them as structured execution traces, and replays them in a strictly isolated environment with zero outbound network access. We formalise the agent execution model, define the request-key matching function K(s), and prove the determinism invariant. We introduce a noise-aware diff algorithm classifying HTTP header divergence into signal and noise tiers. Empirical evaluation across five workloads (n = 250 replay instances) demonstrates replay fidelity F = 1.0 and a median per-step latency reduction of 98.3%. agrepl is implemented in Go, ships as a single static binary, and is released under the MIT licence.
  Keywords: AI agents, deterministic replay, LLM debugging, reproducibility, MITM proxy, execution tracing, record/replay systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces agrepl, a CLI tool for creating deterministic replays of AI agent executions, aiming to resolve non-determinism issues in such systems.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agent systems suffer from non-determinism caused by various factors such as LLM sampling, external APIs, and infrastructure noise, making it impossible to faithfully reproduce prior executions.
AI.method: The paper proposes agrepl, a tool that uses a man-in-the-middle proxy to intercept all interactions, serializes execution traces, and replays them in an isolated, offline environment for deterministic debugging.
AI.result: Experiments across five workloads with 250 replay instances demonstrated perfect replay fidelity (F = 1.0) and achieved a median per-step latency reduction of 98.3%.
AI.conclusion: Agrepl enables deterministic debugging of AI agent systems, ensuring reproducibility, reducing latency, and enhancing observability. It is available as a lightweight Go binary under the MIT license.

title: From Memory to Skills: Evidence-Grounded Co-Evolution Governance for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Existing memory systems for long-horizon LLM agents often retrieve prior traces as passive context rather than converting them into executable capabilities. In this paper, we propose MSCE, a training-free Memory--Skill Co-Evolution framework that organizes agent experience into grounded step traces, reusable procedural policies, and declarative environmental cognition. MSCE crystallizes evidence-backed L2 policies with positive estimated gain into callable skills that retain evidence links, applicability boundaries, decision guidance, verification rules, and reliability estimates. It further introduces reflection-weighted value backfilling, which propagates sparse terminal feedback through dense local self-reflections to produce evidence-calibrated trace values for governing memory and skill evolution. Experiments on EvoAgentBench and LoCoMo demonstrate that MSCE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art skill-augmented and memory-driven agent baselines, exhibiting strong cross-domain transferability and lifelong-evolution capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MSCE, a framework for organizing long-horizon LLM agent experiences into executable skills rather than passive context. MSCE evolves memory and skills while outperforming existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing memory systems passively retrieve traces which limit capability execution and reuse; an advanced system could enhance performance and learning in LLM agents.
AI.method: The MSCE framework organizes agent experiences into step-by-step traces, procedural policies, and environmental cognition. It develops reusable skills with evidence links, boundaries, and reliability. It also uses reflection-weighted value backfilling to improve feedback propagation.
AI.result: MSCE achieves superior performance compared to skill-augmented and memory-driven baselines, with demonstrated cross-domain adaptability and capability for lifelong skill evolution.
AI.conclusion: MSCE successfully advances LLM agent evolution by crystallizing procedural skills and improving feedback mechanisms, enabling effective lifelong learning and skill transfer.

title: ReqGenX: An Empirical Study of Atomic Decomposition, Artifact Regeneration, and Reconstruction for Legacy SRS Documents
comment: Accepted at the International Symposium on Empirical Software Engineering and Measurement (ESEM) technical papers (main) track 2026
summary: Background: Evaluating automated Software Requirements Specification (SRS) generation is challenging because few datasets provide fine-grained traceability between source requirements, intermediate elicitation artifacts, and generated specifications. Aims: We aim to study whether legacy SRS documents can be transformed into traceable synthetic pre-SRS artifacts that support fine-grained evaluation of LLM-based SRS generation. Method: We conduct an empirical study using ReqGenX, a controlled pipeline that decomposes SRS sections into source-grounded atomic statements, routes atoms to standards-inspired artifact types through multi-LLM plurality voting, and generates artifacts using constrained prompts with iterative judge-guided refinement. We evaluate ReqGenX on seven PURE SRS documents using grounding, quality, information retention, and downstream reconstruction analyses. Results: ReqGenX produces faithful and usable atoms, with median AlignScore values typically between 0.96 and 0.99 and Prometheus scores ranging from 4.34 to 4.85. Generated artifacts remain strongly grounded in their source atoms, with AlignScore values typically between 0.80--0.94 and judge pass rates near 100%; stricter Prometheus evaluation yields pass rates from 54.8% to 97.1%. In a downstream SRS reconstruction case study, artifact-backed atoms remain recoverable from generated SRSs, with SBERT means between 0.69 and 0.75 and AlignScore medians between 0.76 and 0.84. Conclusions: Traceable synthetic pre-SRS artifacts can support more fine-grained evaluation of LLM-based SRS generation, while exposing tradeoffs among faithfulness, information retention, and artifact completeness.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method to transform legacy Software Requirement Specification (SRS) documents into traceable artifacts for better evaluation of automatic SRS generation systems.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the lack of datasets with fine-grained traceability between legacy SRS documents and generated specifications. This gap hinders the accurate evaluation of large language model (LLM)-based SRS generation.
AI.method: The paper introduces ReqGenX, a structured pipeline that converts SRS documents into traceable atomic statements. These atoms are organized into artifact types using multi-LLM plurality voting and refined through iterative prompts. Seven SRS documents are empirically tested using various benchmarks such as grounding, quality, and downstream reconstruction.
AI.result: Results show that the pipeline generates accurate artifacts with high AlignScores (0.96–0.99) and Prometheus scores (4.34–4.85), while retaining grounding and recoverability. Even stricter evaluations yield robust results, with partial degradation in pass rates under stricter criteria.
AI.conclusion: Traceable synthetic artifacts can substantially improve the fine-grained evaluation of LLM-based SRS generation tools, although trade-offs exist between faithfulness, information retention, and comprehensiveness.

title: A Causal Markov Condition for Value
summary: This paper proposes a causal independence principle for value -- the value Causal Markov Condition (v-CMC) -- and develops the conceptual and mathematical foundations of a "causal value theory" linking causality and utility. After motivating a local formulation of the v-CMC, we introduce a probability-value duality that translates standard causal-inference results into the value setting. In particular, we formulate local, global, and decomposition versions of the v-CMC and prove their equivalence. We also define v-separation and show that it is sound and complete for conditional value independence. Furthermore, we derive a Bellman-type recursion as a special case of the v-CMC, thereby generalizing standard Bellman recursion from linear chains to causal DAGs. Finally, we show how the v-CMC supports modular transfer and updating of utility information across causal contexts and develop algorithms for causally structured utility elicitation and canonical influence-diagram construction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the value Causal Markov Condition (v-CMC) as a framework connecting causality and utility, formulates its theoretical foundations, and develops algorithms leveraging this relationship.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper lies in advancing the understanding of how causality interplays with utility in decision-making processes and providing a principled mathematical framework to address this interaction.
AI.method: The paper formulates the value Causal Markov Condition (v-CMC), defines a probability-value duality to adapt causal inference to the value domain, develops theoretical constructs such as v-separation, and derives a Bellman-type recursion for causal structures.
AI.result: Several key results are achieved, including proving the equivalence of local, global, and decomposition versions of v-CMC, deriving algorithms for causally structured utility elicitation, and generalizing Bellman recursion for causal decision structures.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes the v-CMC as a robust theoretical model linking causality and utility, creating new tools for modular updating and decision-making in causally structured systems.

title: Back to the museum: Investigation of the acceptance of Android Andrea with and without emotion simulation in a museum
comment: Presented at the ACM Intelligent Virtual Agents conference in 2026 (IVA2026)
summary: For a second time, the android robot Andrea was set up at a public museum in Germany for six consecutive days to have conversations with visitors, fully autonomously. Building on previously gathered qualitative results, the robot was now capable of engaging in multi-lingual conversation with the visitors about the museum context. The robot was prepared with context information about the museum in general and its surrounding exhibits this time. The robot featured a slightly artificial sounding voice that was previously evaluated as congruent with its gender-ambiguous but very humanlike design. Three experimental conditions were implemented, in which either (1) the robot simulated no emotions, (2) the robots emotions were determined by ChatGPT 4.1, or (3) the WASABI emotion simulation architecture simulated the robot's emotion dynamics. An extended version of the TAM2 questionnaire was employed to let 73 visitors report on several factors of their opinion about the android robot Andrea after having experienced it. In result, the statistical analysis suggests that these first two approaches to implementing emotions into the chat architecture of our android robot Andrea did not yield any positive effects on the subjective evaluations by the visitors and were not detectable on a conscious level.

AI.tldr: This study implemented emotional simulation into the conversations of the android robot Andrea at a museum but found no noticeable effects on visitor evaluations.
AI.motivation: To enhance the interactive experience of visitors with the android robot Andrea at a museum by implementing emotional and multilingual conversational capabilities.
AI.method: The robot was equipped with museum-related conversational content and conducted experiments under three conditions: no emotions, ChatGPT 4.1-driven emotions, and WASABI emotion simulation. Visitor feedback was collected using an extended TAM2 questionnaire.
AI.result: Statistical analysis revealed that the emotional aspects implemented did not positively affect visitors' subjective evaluations or conscious perceptions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating emotional dynamics using current methods (ChatGPT 4.1 or WASABI) into the robot's conversation did not improve user experience or evaluations.

title: Moving Like a Human: Ego-Motion-Normalized Temporal Signatures for Real-Time Aerial Person Tracking on Milliwatt-Class Hardware
summary: Follow-me person tracking must run on the drone itself, where affordable companion computers offer only a few effective int8 GFLOP/s. At typical follow distances a person spans 10-60 pixels, indistinguishable from clutter and beyond the reach of single-frame appearance detectors. The missing evidence is temporal and belongs in the input representation, computed analytically, rather than in learned temporal machinery. EMTS-Det is a five-stage system that estimates ego-motion, converts each frame into ego-motion-normalized residual-motion channels, detects person centers with a 22k-parameter, 7.6-MFLOP network, tracks a locked target with a Kalman filter in stabilized coordinates, and verifies tracks with a 1-D convolutional classifier of human motion (ROC AUC 0.941). Training uses a synthetic-motion curriculum with motion channels generated by the deployed ego-motion code. Multi-seed ablations locate the value in generalization: on held-out VisDrone-DET a luminance-only variant collapses to 0.051 AP25 versus 0.415, as does YOLOv8n fine-tuned identically despite 1,100 times the compute, while the deployed int8 detector reaches 0.694 AP25 in-domain and 0.444 on this split. Temporal-shift modules lower accuracy, so the deployed detector is stateless. Silent int8 calibration failures are documented; min-max calibration with propagated caches matches float within 0.008 AP. On a Raspberry Pi Zero 2W the pipeline runs at 31.85 FPS with 0.462 AP25 and 0.714 recall over 1,000 real-world UAV videos, versus 1.95 FPS and 0.172 AP25 for YOLOv8n. A 57-second field sequence shows auto-lock at 1.3 s, 97.9% lock recall, and recovery from all nine occlusions with zero false re-locks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EMTS-Det, a lightweight follow-me person tracking system for drones, achieving robust performance on low-compute platforms like the Raspberry Pi Zero 2W.
AI.motivation: Current follow-me person tracking systems face challenges due to the limited computational capacity of drones and the difficulty of distinguishing small-scale targets from background clutter, especially at significant distances.
AI.method: The proposed system, EMTS-Det, employs a five-stage pipeline that includes ego-motion estimation, residual-motion analysis, a lightweight person detection network, Kalman filter-based tracking, and a 1-D motion classifier for verification. Training is based on synthetic-motion data, while optimization focuses on low computational overhead.
AI.result: EMTS-Det achieves significantly higher accuracy and speed compared to YOLOv8n, with 31.85 FPS and 0.462 AP25 on a Raspberry Pi Zero 2W, demonstrating reliability in real-world and synthetic scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The lightweight design and temporal feature-based approach of EMTS-Det make it highly effective for real-time, low-compute drone-based person tracking, addressing current challenges of resource constraints and performance.

title: RouteCost: A Production-Inspired Multi-Stage Framework for Pre-Order Shipping Cost Estimation in E-Commerce
summary: Accurate pre-order shipping cost estimation is important in e-commerce because it affects price presentation, margin planning, and conversion. In practice, shipping cost is shaped not only by distance but also by destination demand mix, billable weight, dimensional pricing, surcharge triggers, and latent operational effects such as shipment consolidation. Static lookup methods therefore miss important sources of variation, while monolithic regressors may exploit strong but non-causal correlations. We propose RouteCost, a production-inspired multi-stage framework that decomposes the problem into time-aware demand forecasting, fee-card-informed baseline pricing, Stage 2 residual correction, and proxy-based box-consolidation inference. Route-level cost estimates are aggregated through a route-weighted expectation formulation to produce product-level shipping cost predictions. Across over 250,000 orders, 260 products, and 18 months of order history, the framework improves predictive quality and aggregate calibration while preserving route-level interpretability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RouteCost, a multi-stage framework for accurate e-commerce shipping cost prediction, improving quality and interpretability over traditional methods.
AI.motivation: To improve pre-order shipping cost estimation in e-commerce, where static methods and monolithic models fail to capture the complexities affecting shipping costs.
AI.method: The approach decomposes the task into time-aware demand forecasting, fee-card-informed pricing, residual correction, and box-consolidation inference, aggregated via a route-weighted expectation method.
AI.result: Across 250,000+ orders, 260 products, and 18 months of data, the framework demonstrated better predictive accuracy, improved calibration, and maintained interpretability.
AI.conclusion: RouteCost provides a scalable, interpretable, and effective solution for shipping cost estimation, addressing variation sources overlooked by other methods.

title: ThAME: 3D Memory-Enabled Heterogeneous Accelerator for LLM Mixture of Experts
comment: Accepted for Publication in IEEE/ACM Embedded Systems Week (ESWEEK-26)
summary: Mixture of Experts (MoE) architectures have emerged as a dominant paradigm for scaling Large Language Models (LLMs). However, MoE inference on conventional hardware is constrained by three fundamental bottlenecks. These encompass the massive memory bandwidth required to fetch non-contiguous expert weights, the non-deterministic scatter-gather traffic generated by input-dependent token routing, and the tail-latency dependency imposed by synchronous expert output aggregation. To address these challenges, we propose ThAME, a three-dimensional (3D) heterogeneous multi-chiplet architecture for MoE inference. ThAME employs Ferroelectric Field-Effect Transistor (FeFET)-based non-volatile and DRAM-based volatile memory chiplets with a co-designed compute mapping strategy that aligns the distinct computational profiles of attention mechanisms and expert routing. Furthermore, we design a specialized Network-on-Chip communication backbone optimized to mitigate the bottlenecks associated with non-deterministic token routing traffic across the combinatorial space of input-dependent MoE traffic patterns. Experimental results demonstrate that ThAME outperforms state-of-the-art counterparts by up to 15.7x in terms of speedup and improves energy efficiency by up to 9.8x.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ThAME, a novel architecture targeting bottlenecks in running Mixture of Experts (MoE) models on conventional hardware, achieving significant speedup and energy improvements.
AI.motivation: Mixture of Experts architectures are efficient for scaling Large Language Models but are hindered by challenges in hardware inference, including memory bottlenecks, routing inefficiencies, and latency dependencies.
AI.method: The authors introduce ThAME, a 3D heterogeneous multi-chiplet system using non-volatile FeFET and volatile DRAM memory chiplets. It features a custom compute mapping strategy and an optimized Network-on-Chip communication to address routing and memory access inefficiencies.
AI.result: ThAME achieves up to 15.7x speedup and 9.8x improvement in energy efficiency compared to state-of-the-art hardware systems for MoE inference.
AI.conclusion: ThAME shows notable performance and energy improvements in addressing the inherent bottlenecks of MoE inference on conventional hardware, demonstrating the potential of heterogeneous multi-chiplet architectures.

title: Online path planning for kinematic-constrained UAVs in a dynamic environment based on a Differential Evolution algorithm
comment: Accepted to the 40th Anniversary of the IEEE Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA@40)
summary: This research presents an online path planner for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) that can handle dynamic obstacles and UAV motion constraints, including maximum curvature and desired orientations. Our proposed planner uses a NURBS path representation and a Differential Evolution algorithm, incorporating concepts from the Velocity Obstacle approach in a constraint function. Initial results show that our approach is feasible and provides a foundation for future extensions to three-dimensional (3D) environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an online path planner for UAVs that tackles dynamic obstacles and motion constraints using NURBS paths and Differential Evolution.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of creating a robust online path planning solution for UAVs that can adapt to dynamic obstacles and motion constraints.
AI.method: The approach involves using NURBS paths combined with a Differential Evolution algorithm, integrated with concepts from the Velocity Obstacle method for constraint handling.
AI.result: Initial results demonstrate the feasibility of the approach and suggest potential for further development into 3D environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed planner is effective in dynamic and motion-constrained scenarios, paving the way for enhanced UAV path planning in the future.

title: Weakly Non-Negative Supermartingales for Omega-Regular Verification
summary: Martingale-based methods are central to probabilistic program verification, but strong global non-negativity requirements can exclude simple certificates from tractable template classes. Relaxing this requirement enlarges the search space for automated synthesis, but naive relaxations are unsound in the probabilistic setting. We introduce lazy Streett supermartingales and their lexicographic extension, showing that weak non-negativity can nevertheless be used soundly to certify almost-sure satisfaction of $ω$-regular properties with polynomial templates under a broad class of sampling distributions, including all bounded-support distributions. This extends prior weakly non-negative methods from termination to general $ω$-regular verification. We further give a compositional account of lexicographic certificates in terms of one-dimensional ones. Experiments on 170 polynomial probabilistic-program benchmarks show increases of 20.0-23.5 percentage points in verification success over the strongly non-negative baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces lazy Streett supermartingales to relax non-negativity in probabilistic program verification, enabling sound certification of ω-regular properties with improved success rates.
AI.motivation: Probabilistic program verification methods often rely on martingales, but strong global non-negativity requirements narrow the search space for automated synthesis. By relaxing this requirement, the paper seeks to expand the scope of tractable templates and improve verification outcomes.
AI.method: The authors propose lazy Streett supermartingales and their lexicographic extension to enable weak non-negativity, which is sound for certifying almost-sure satisfaction of ω-regular properties. They also present a compositional framework for lexicographic certificates and verify their approach through experiments.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on 170 benchmarks shows that the method improves verification success rates by 20.0-23.5 percentage points compared to strongly non-negative approaches.
AI.conclusion: The relaxation of non-negativity via lazy Streett supermartingales broadens the applicability of probabilistic program verification to general ω-regular properties and achieves significant improvements in verification success.

title: Beyond Storage: State as a Runtime Control Problem in Parallel and Distributed Systems
comment: 53 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to ACM Computing Surveys
summary: Shared state increasingly shapes both performance and failure behavior in streaming, serving, retrieval, and continual-learning systems. Existing studies, however, often isolate access control, hardware-aware execution, memory management, and long-horizon updates. The review organizes this literature around three coupled dimensions: state access and scheduling, state-aware execution, and state evolution and reuse. Across these dimensions, the literature is synthesized through a common scaffold: state object, control surface, coupling path, evaluation boundary, and unresolved contract. This comparison identifies recurring anti-patterns and informs a contract-oriented blueprint and disturbance-aware evaluation agenda. Taken together, the evidence characterizes state management as a runtime control problem.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews and organizes literature on shared-state management in systems, identifies issues, and introduces a blueprint for addressing challenges.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the fragmented understanding of shared state interactions in complex systems by synthesizing literature across access, execution, and evolution dimensions.
AI.method: They review and synthesize existing research through a framework focusing on components like state objects and control interfaces, highlighting recurring challenges and gaps.
AI.result: A contract-oriented blueprint and disturbance-aware evaluation agenda are proposed to better understand and manage shared state.
AI.conclusion: Shared state management is conceptualized as a runtime control problem, and the study offers actionable insights into recurring design challenges.

title: Generative Ontology Induction: Domain-Agnostic Schema Discovery from Document Corpora Using Large Language Models
summary:
Ontology engineering remains a critical bottleneck in knowledge-intensive AI systems. Existing automated approaches either depend on predefined schemas, operate within narrow domains, or produce unstructured outputs unsuitable for downstream pipelines.
  We introduce Generative Ontology Induction (GOI), a domain-agnostic framework that induces a generative blueprint - entities, dimensions, properties, relationships, and constraints - from a corpus of examples and exports it as a typed graph (six node types, seven edge types) in YAML/JSON. We introduce the Node Coverage Score, a novel evaluation metric that measures the fraction of structural ontology nodes (classes, properties, and dimensions) appearing in generated outputs.
  A controlled generative validation on four contrasting ontologies - a familiar Software Services Invoice schema, a custom Job Description Ontology, a confidential Pain-Management Clinical Visit Record Ontology, and a Professional Services Contract & Statement of Work Ontology - shows that GOI-prompted generation covers 95-100% of the structural backbone in every case; a generic three-field template holds at 97.8% on the invoice schema but drops to 52.2% on the Job Description Ontology, 62.2% on the Pain-Management ontology, and 78.3% on the Professional Services Contract ontology. The structural coverage holds regardless of how familiar the document type is to the model.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Generative Ontology Induction (GOI), a framework for automated ontology generation that overcomes limitations such as reliance on predefined schemas and narrow domain focus. It generates structured, domain-agnostic ontologies validated across various examples.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in ontology engineering caused by reliance on predefined schemas, narrow domain constraints, and unstructured outputs in automated approaches.
AI.method: The GOI framework generates a structured ontology blueprint from example corpora and exports it as a typed graph, evaluated using the Node Coverage Score metric.
AI.result: Controlled generative validation demonstrates that GOI achieves 95-100% structural backbone coverage across four diverse test ontologies regardless of model familiarity with document types.
AI.conclusion: GOI is effective in generating comprehensive structural ontologies across diverse domains, overcoming limitations of existing approaches and providing high coverage across various examples.

title: OpenLanguageModel: Readable and Composable Small-Language-Model Pretraining for Education and Research
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, and 1 table. Website: https://openlanguagemodel.github.io/openlanguagemodel/. Code: https://github.com/openlanguagemodel/openlanguagemodel
summary: OpenLanguageModel (OLM) is an open-source PyTorch library for building and pretraining small language models while keeping their machinery visible. In OLM, model code reads like the architecture: components are ordinary modules, while Block, Residual, Repeat, and Parallel describe how they are wired. The resulting model can move unchanged from a teaching notebook to a complete pretraining run or a research ablation. OLM connects this readable model layer to tokenizers, local and streaming datasets, optimization, mixed precision, callbacks, checkpoints, and hardware-aware CPU, single-GPU, and single-node multi-GPU execution. We demonstrate the full path by tracing GPT-2 from diagram to code, launching a FineWeb-Edu training script, replacing one attention component, and letting AutoTrainer configure the available machine. The package includes 27 presets across nine familiar model families and documentation that progresses from LM fundamentals to architecture research. Validation shows close agreement with independent reference implementations, 90.6% four-GPU weak-scaling efficiency for a 348M-parameter workload, compact architecture edits, and positive early usability results. OLM is MIT-licensed and available through PyPI, GitHub, and its documentation site.

AI.tldr: OLM is an open-source library designed for developing and pretraining small language models, offering visibility into model architecture and mechanisms.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to simplify the creation, training, and modification of small language models by providing a readable and accessible structure for researchers and developers.
AI.method: The library uses PyTorch to integrate clear modular designs for models with components like Block, Residual, Repeat, and Parallel, while supporting tokenization, datasets, optimization, and GPU execution.
AI.result: OLM provides 27 presets, close performance validation, 90.6% weak-scaling efficiency on a multi-GPU setup, and promising usability outcomes in early testing.
AI.conclusion: OLM bridges the gap between teaching tools and large-scale pretraining environments, enabling both educational and research applications with a highly accessible framework.

title: From Discussion to Execution: Replicating Buggy and Correct Data Science Code
comment: accepted at the 37th IEEE International Symposium on Software Reliability Engineering (ISSRE 2026)
summary: Reproducing reliable data science code from informal sources is challenging due to ambiguous problem specifications, missing dependencies, and performance bottlenecks. Although developer Q&A forums provide rich discussions on diagnosing and fixing real-world issues, the information is often incomplete and unstructured, limiting its use for automated debugging and verification. In this paper, we introduce Reprodgen, a large language model (LLM) based framework for automatically replicating executable buggy and patched data science programs from Q&A forum posts. Given a question and its corresponding answer, Reprodgen reconstructs the buggy behavior described in the question and the intended fix described in the answer, producing executable buggy and patched code pairs that reflect the original discussion. The framework builds structured representations of code intent (CI), functional requirements (FR), and Structured Chain of Thought (SCoT), and iteratively refines code using an LLM-based reviewer until it is executable and semantically consistent. We evaluate Reprodgen on Stack Overflow (SO) and GitHub Issues (GI) across seven data science libraries, including pandas, numpy, and scikit-learn, and construct a benchmark of runnable buggy and patched programs validated by human experts. Our pipeline uses LLMs for semantic assessment, while executability is verified through actual execution. Results show reliable replication with clear differences in model performance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Reprodgen, a framework using large language models to replicate buggy and patched data science code from Q&A forums like Stack Overflow.
AI.motivation: Reproducing code from Q&A forums is difficult due to vague problem descriptions, incomplete dependencies, and performance hurdles, which limits automated debugging and code verification.
AI.method: Reprodgen leverages LLMs to iteratively reconstruct buggy and patched programs by building structured representations (CI, FR, SCoT) and using LLM-based reviewers for refinement until the code is executable and semantically accurate.
AI.result: The framework was tested on forums like Stack Overflow and GitHub Issues across seven data science libraries, producing runnable code pairs verified by experts. The results demonstrated its reliability and highlighted performance differences between models.
AI.conclusion: Reprodgen shows promise in automating executable code reproduction and debugging from informal discussions, advancing data science and programming workflows.

title: Semi-Supervised Conditional Generative Learning through Stochastic Interpolation and Sufficient Representations
summary: Conditional generative modeling remains a challenging problem in semi-supervised settings where labeled data is scarce but unlabeled samples are abundant. To effectively leverage structural information embedded within the unlabeled dataset and compensate for sparse conditioning signals, we propose a semi-supervised framework combining conditional stochastic interpolation with low-dimensional latent representations. RepG decomposes generation into two stages: label-dependent latent sampling and high-dimensional reconstruction. This isolates the supervised learning of conditional dependencies to a low-dimensional space, requiring few labels while utilizing the abundant unlabeled data purely for reconstruction. Theoretically, we establish an error decomposition showing that the Kullback-Leibler divergence of RepG comprises stage-wise estimation errors and a structural bias quantified by conditional mutual information. For deep neural network estimators, we derive non-asymptotic convergence rates proving that RepG significantly improves sample complexity. By confining the supervised estimation burden to the low intrinsic dimension of the latent representation, RepG achieves a strictly faster convergence rate. Complemented by a minimax lower bound, our theoretical results demonstrate that this method effectively mitigates the curse of dimensionality inherent in direct ambient-space generative modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RepG, a semi-supervised generative model that separates conditional dependency learning into two stages to effectively use limited labels and abundant unlabeled data.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges in conditional generative modeling when labeled data is scarce but unlabeled data is plentiful by leveraging structural information in the data.
AI.method: RepG framework decomposes generation into two stages: a label-dependent latent sampling step followed by a high-dimensional reconstruction, isolating supervised learning to a low-dimensional latent space.
AI.result: RepG theoretically improves sample complexity with faster convergence rates and mitigates dimensionality challenges, supported by non-asymptotic rate analysis and minimax lower bounds.
AI.conclusion: RepG is a robust solution to semi-supervised conditional generative modeling, enhancing efficiency by focusing supervised learning on low-dimensional latent spaces.

title: Linear Stability Analysis of an INDI Pitch-Rate Controller under Model Mismatch for a Tilt-Rotor VTOL UAV
summary: Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) is attractive for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) flight control because it reduces dependence on a full aerodynamic model while retaining strong disturbance-rejection capability. For a tilt-rotor vertical takeoff and landing (VTOL) architecture, however, the admissible model-mismatch range of the fast inner loop is still not characterized analytically in a parameter-explicit way. This paper isolates the pitch-rate/elevon subchannel of an existing cascaded INDI controller and studies its linear stability under model mismatch. A closed-form fifth-order transfer function is derived for the full controller-estimator-actuator-plant interconnection, and stability is characterized through the Routh-Hurwitz criterion over a parameterized linear model. Two representative three-parameter sweeps produce interpretable stability regions. Based on these feasibility maps, two uncertainty-aware tuning procedures are proposed: a robustness-oriented design that maximizes a weighted worst-case combination of gain margin and phase margin, and a performance-oriented design that maximizes worst-case closed-loop bandwidth subject to margin constraints. The results show that actuator lag and inertia mismatch are comparatively benign at nominal gain, whereas control-effectiveness mismatch, particularly a sign error in the allocation, is the most dangerous destabilizing factor, leading to concrete tuning recommendations for conservative and aggressive operating conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the stability of Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) flight control for tilt-rotor UAVs under model mismatch and proposes tuning strategies to enhance robustness and performance.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of an analytical characterization of the stability of INDI control under model mismatch in tilt-rotor UAVs, focusing on parameter-explicit stability characterization.
AI.method: The pitch-rate/elevon subchannel of a cascaded INDI controller is isolated, and its linear stability under model mismatch is analyzed using a derived fifth-order transfer function and the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion.
AI.result: Stability regions are mapped using parameterized linear models, and two tuning methods are proposed to enhance robustness or performance. Actuator lag and inertia mismatch are shown to have limited destabilizing effects, while control-effectiveness mismatch is identified as the key destabilizer.
AI.conclusion: The study offers concrete tuning recommendations to optimize fly-controller settings for either conservative or aggressive operating conditions in tilt-rotor UAVs.

title: GenSyn10: A Multi-Generative AI Dataset For Benchmarking Image Classification
summary: The rapid advancement of generative AI has outpaced our ability to reliably detect its outputs, particularly when detectors encounter generators they have not seen before. We introduce GenSyn10, a CIFAR-10-aligned synthetic image dataset of 60,000 images (10 classes, 32$\times$32, 50k/10k split) generated using three architecturally diverse state-of-the-art models: FLUX.2-dev (Rectified Flow Transformer), HunyuanImage-3.0 (MoE Transformer), and Qwen-Image-2512 (Multimodal Diffusion Transformer), to advance research in AI-generated image detection. A central challenge in this domain is that detectors perform well on known generators but degrade on unseen ones. GenSyn10 addresses this limitation by curating data from multiple contemporary architectures under a standardized generation protocol, enabling controlled and systematic evaluation of out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization to novel generators. Images are generated using a template-based prompt engine and downsampled to ensure consistency. We evaluate 17 image classification models under a four-stage protocol: real-data baseline, zero-shot transfer, fine-tuning, and retention. Despite a measurable domain gap, CIFAR-10-trained models achieve up to 96.86\% zero-shot accuracy on GenSyn10, increasing to 99.88\% after fine-tuning. In binary real-vs-synthetic classification, fine-tuned models achieve 97-99.9\% accuracy on seen generators but drop to 79-96\% on images from an unseen generator, highlighting persistent limitations in OOD generalization. These results establish GenSyn10 as a controlled benchmark for studying synthetic image detection beyond single-generator settings, supporting research on robustness, domain adaptation, and cross-generator generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GenSyn10, a dataset of synthetic images to study challenges in detecting AI-generated outputs, especially for unseen generators.
AI.motivation: Generative AI advancements have made it difficult to detect its outputs, particularly for previously unseen generators, necessitating robust benchmarks.
AI.method: GenSyn10 consists of 60,000 CIFAR-10-aligned images created by three diverse state-of-the-art generation models under a standardized protocol, evaluated through a four-stage testing process.
AI.result: Models achieved high accuracy (up to 99.88% after fine-tuning) for detecting synthetic images from known generators but showed significant accuracy drops (79-96%) for unseen ones.
AI.conclusion: GenSyn10 provides a valuable benchmark for studying detection robustness and domain adaptation across multiple synthetic image generators.

title: Orthogonal Gradient Constraints Shape Noisy-Label Memorization Dynamics
comment: 7 pages, accepted into the ICML HiLD workshop 2026 @Seoul
summary: Modern neural networks can fit corrupted training labels, making noisy-label learning a useful setting for studying memorization-driven overfitting. Most regularization methods modify the objective, architecture, or data distribution; here we instead study a geometric intervention on the optimizer update itself. We evaluate OrthoGrad, which removes the component of each weight gradient parallel to the current weight vector, in noisy-label image classification. On MNIST with small-data regimes, OrthoGrad improves test accuracy most clearly for CNNs while reducing corrupted-label fitting. Mechanism diagnostics based on weight norms and gradient-weight cosine similarity suggest that the projection has the strongest effect when the raw gradient contains a nontrivial radial component, and becomes weaker in larger-data regimes where gradients are already nearly orthogonal to weights. Additional CIFAR-10 ResNet-18 experiments show that the method can alter memorization trajectories but does not prevent eventual noisy-label memorization. These results support orthogonal update constraints as a useful diagnostic for studying learning dynamics, while showing that OrthoGrad is regime-dependent rather than universally regularizing.

AI.tldr: This study explores OrthoGrad, a method that modifies the optimizer in neural networks by removing gradient components parallel to weight vectors, to regulate noisy-label learning. Results show improved performance in small-data scenarios, though it doesn't fully prevent memorization in larger datasets.
AI.motivation: Understanding how neural networks overfit noisy-label data and identifying mechanisms to prevent memorization-driven overfitting in such scenarios.
AI.method: The study proposes OrthoGrad, a geometric modification to optimization processes that excludes gradient components parallel to weight vectors during updates. Experiments are conducted using MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets.
AI.result: OrthoGrad improves test accuracy in small-data MNIST experiments with CNNs by lowering the fitting of corrupted labels. On larger CIFAR-10 datasets, it alters memorization dynamics but does not prevent eventual noisy-label overfitting.
AI.conclusion: Orthogonal update constraints like OrthoGrad offer insights into learning dynamics and are effective in specific regimes, but they are not universal solutions for noisy-label regularization across varied data scales.

title: ThRIve: Thermally Robust CNN Inference via Low-Rank Adaptation in Heterogeneous PIM Architectures
comment: Published in ACM Transactions on Design Automation of Electronic Systems, Volume 31, Issue 2
summary: Processing-In-Memory (PIM) has emerged as a promising technology for accelerating machine learning (ML) workloads. Specifically, non-volatile memory-based PIM architectures have enabled effective ML acceleration due to their ability to perform energy-efficient matrix-vector multiplication operations. However, these devices suffer from non-idealities such as thermal noise. This noise alters the stored values in the memory cells which correspond to actual model weights, compromising the inference accuracy. In this work, we introduce ThRIve, a noise-aware training methodology that leverages low-rank adaptation to enable thermally robust inference on heterogeneous PIM architectures. ThRIve selectively stores these low-rank noise-aware parameters on a hardware that is less susceptible to thermal noise, enabling robustness against temperature-induced noise variations. ThRIve mitigates the effects of thermal-noise and prevent the drop in inference accuracy across the entire operating temperature range. Experimental results demonstrate that ThRIve-enabled architectures maintain consistent inference accuracy, with the mean accuracy staying within 2% of the ideal (i.e., noise-free) accuracy, and the variation in accuracy across the entire operating temperature range remaining within 2% of the mean. The proposed methodology achieves accuracy and robustness comparable to thermally-resilient Static Random-Access Memory (SRAM)-based PIM systems, while delivering up to 5.4x reduction in energy-delay product (EDP) during CNN model inferencing.

AI.tldr: ThRIve is a methodology for non-volatile memory-based PIM architectures that mitigates thermal noise to ensure robust inference for ML workloads.
AI.motivation: Thermal noise in non-volatile PIM architectures compromises inference accuracy, necessitating approaches to ensure robustness for machine learning accelerations.
AI.method: ThRIve employs noise-aware training through low-rank adaptation, selectively storing thermally robust model parameters in less susceptible hardware.
AI.result: ThRIve maintains inference accuracy within 2% of ideal values across operating temperatures while achieving up to 5.4x energy-delay product reduction during CNN inference.
AI.conclusion: ThRIve delivers thermally robust PIM-based ML acceleration comparable in accuracy to SRAM systems, with significant energy-delay efficiency improvements.

title: Discovery by Dreaming: Cross-Domain Recombination in Artificial Memory
summary: Dreams splice together people, places, and times that never met. Neuroscience suggests this recombination is not noise, but a function driving insight and creative discovery. This reframes memory consolidation: rather than merely defending against forgetting, its measurable value lies in recombining knowledge across experiences that have not yet co-occurred. We test this directly by isolating the recombinatory-replay mechanism and implementing it in two architecturally unrelated systems: a LoRA fine-tuning pipeline (DREAMS) and a symbolic engine replaying structured knowledge objects (SAPIENCE). Both systems converge on the same finding: cross-domain consolidation creates value, while within-domain rehearsal does not. The symbolic arm surfaces novel cross-domain connections at 85.7%, a +21 percentage point (pp) gain over baseline. The neural arm improves overall by +5.64 pp, but on subtasks explicitly requiring cross-domain transfer (like unseen math reasoning on GSM8K), gains reach +14.5 pp. This effect is a genuine property of the weights--not a prompt artifact--as prepending the same material in-context to a 671B-parameter model actually reverses the gain. We validate this prediction against documented discoveries across 50,000 real papers and state a falsifiable hippocampal-recording prediction to distinguish recombination from rehearsal. Ultimately, this principle is substrate-general, tracking real discovery at scale. Reading the literature teaches a model to recall what it has seen, but producing discovery requires a separate offline phase that recombines knowledge across domains--the computational analog of dreaming. Consolidation is not for remembering, but for discovering.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the recombinatory-replay mechanism in memory consolidation, proposing it as essential for cross-domain insights and creative discovery, validated through two systems (DREAMS and SAPIENCE).
AI.motivation: Understanding memory consolidation's role in discovering cross-domain connections rather than just preserving information.
AI.method: The authors leverage a LoRA fine-tuning pipeline (DREAMS) and a symbolic replay engine (SAPIENCE) to test recombinatory mechanisms.
AI.result: Both systems reveal cross-domain consolidation significantly improves discovery, with gains of up to +21 pp in the symbolic arm and +14.5 pp on neural subtasks requiring transfer.
AI.conclusion: Consolidation drives discovery through recombination rather than preservation, suggesting an offline domain-crossing phase akin to dreaming for innovation.

title: Parameterized Verification of Deterministic MPI Programs
summary: We consider the problem of verifying a message passing program in which the number of processes is a parameter NP and each process knows its unique ID. Processes communicate using send and receive commands which specify a single destination or source. To verify the program, the user provides functions specifying the number of messages sent from process i to process j, the level of each communication event in the happens-before hierarchy, and a fact that holds for the k-th message sent from i to j. These are used to transform the program to a parameterized sequential program which can be verified using any techniques appropriate for such programs. We realize this approach in an extension to Frama-C/Wp to verify C/MPI programs.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses verifying message-passing programs with a parameterized number of processes using transformations to sequential programs.
AI.motivation: To enable the verification of message-passing programs parameterized by the number of processes, which rely on send and receive communications.
AI.method: Transforms message-passing programs into sequential programs using user-provided functions specifying communication details, then verifies with Frama-C/Wp.
AI.result: Facilitates verification of C/MPI programs by modeling complex communication in a sequential manner, improving usability of existing tools.
AI.conclusion: Proposed approach simplifies the verification of parameterized message-passing programs and extends the capabilities of Frama-C/Wp.

title: EdgeCoInfer: Hierarchical Collaborative Inference for On-Device Multimodal Large Models
summary: Modern mobile applications predominantly execute concurrent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to provide ubiquitous intelligence. However, satisfying this demand within edge environments faces significant challenges due to multi-task concurrency and strictly coupled hard constraints. To address these issues, we propose EdgeCoInfer, a framework enabling granularity-adaptive deployment by co-optimizing inter-model functional module sharing and \textbf{intra-model fine-grained partitioning}. We solve the underlying Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Programming (MINLP) problem via a Hybrid Evolutionary Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HE-HRL) paradigm, which synchronizes a Genetic Algorithm (GA) for discrete model placement with a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) agent for continuous resource allocation. To navigate the sparse feasible region, we introduce a feasibility-guided constructive execution mechanism, integrating a constructive cut-step decoder with pre-act pruning and a two-phase curriculum strategy for stable adaptation. Experimental results demonstrate that EdgeCoInfer ensures a 100\% task completion rate in high-concurrency scenarios, achieving a 76\% reduction in system cost and 71.88\% memory savings compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: EdgeCoInfer is a framework designed to optimize the deployment of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in edge environments by addressing concurrency challenges and resource constraints, achieving significant cost and memory savings.
AI.motivation: The growing demand for running concurrent multimodal large language models on edge devices faces challenges due to hard constraints and high multitask concurrency requirements.
AI.method: The framework employs a hybrid evolutionary hierarchical reinforcement learning (HE-HRL) approach, combining a Genetic Algorithm for model placement with a Soft Actor-Critic agent for resource allocation, and includes a feasibility-guided mechanism for enhanced adaptability.
AI.result: The proposed EdgeCoInfer achieves a 100% task completion rate in high-concurrency scenarios while reducing system costs by 76% and saving 71.88% in memory compared to current methods.
AI.conclusion: EdgeCoInfer significantly enhances the feasibility and efficiency of running multimodal models on edge devices, making it suitable for high-concurrency environments by optimizing both resource utilization and task execution.

title: Democratizing AI with Small Language Models: Structured Benchmarking and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning for Local Deployment
summary: AI democratization is not primarily a question of matching frontier-scale generality; it is a question of whether capable models can be selected, audited, and specialized under hardware and governance constraints that ordinary institutions can actually satisfy. This paper studies that problem through a controlled evaluation of nine open-weight language models between 135M and 3B parameters on a 1,085-example, 16-topic multiple-choice benchmark designed for structured local deployment. The benchmark emphasizes symbolic precision, constrained formatting, extraction, and short-horizon semantic decision making under a strict one-letter output protocol. A shared parameter-efficient fine-tuning pipeline then adapts a subset of models using 4-bit NF4 quantization with DoRA/LoRA-style adapters on an NVIDIA L4-class budget. In base evaluation, Qwen Coder 3B leads at 75.67% strict accuracy, followed by Qwen2.5 1.5B at 67.10%, Qwen3.5 2B at 64.98%, and Granite 3.3 2B at 64.61%. On the shared 108-example held-out fine-tuning split, adaptation improves Qwen Coder 3B by +26.85 points, SmolLM2 1.7B by +25.92, Qwen2.5 1.5B by +19.44, SmolLM2 360M by +10.18, and SmolLM2 135M by +5.55. Across ranking, topic-level heterogeneity, difficulty strata, failure composition, efficiency frontiers, and topic-conditioned transfer, the same conclusion recurs: a disciplined workflow of benchmark construction, cross-model evaluation, and low-cost specialization already makes a subset of sub-3B models viable as local experts for structured niche workloads.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates nine open-weight language models with parameters ranging from 135M to 3B, exploring their utility for structured local deployment using a multiple-choice benchmark and parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategies.
AI.motivation: This study aims to enable the use of AI models in local and specialized settings by optimizing their auditing, selection, and adaptation, under constraints that typical institutions can meet.
AI.method: The authors designed a 1,085-example multiple-choice benchmark focused on structured tasks and fine-tuned select models using efficient methods like 4-bit NF4 quantization and DoRA/LoRA adapters.
AI.result: Qwen Coder 3B achieved the highest base accuracy at 75.67%, and fine-tuning improved the results of multiple models by significant margins, demonstrating the effectiveness of specialized adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Focused and efficient workflows involving benchmarking, evaluation, and fine-tuning can make smaller models under 3B parameters capable of excelling in niche and specialized tasks, even under limited hardware budgets.

title: SpecLA: Efficient Speculative Decoding for Linear-Attention Models
summary: Linear-attention models replace the growing KV cache with recurrent states, but autoregressive decoding still reads, updates, and writes these states one token at a time. Speculative decoding can reduce this cost by verifying several draft tokens in one target pass, yet existing speculative systems are designed for Transformer KV caches. For stateful linear-attention targets, verification must follow recurrent dependencies across chains and branches, acceptance must update only the accepted state trajectory, and the drafter must avoid submitting candidates that waste stateful verification work. This paper presents SpecLA, a speculative decoding runtime for stateful linear-attention models. SpecLA verifies chains and trees with topology-aware kernels, stores compact factors produced during verification to recover accepted states, and uses confidence pruning plus a target-aligned EAGLE-style drafter to feed useful candidates to the verifier. On an NVIDIA H100 with a public GDN-1.3B target, SpecLA achieves up to 1.70x end-to-end speedup over autoregressive decoding.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SpecLA, a speculative decoding mechanism for stateful linear-attention models, which improves decoding efficiency by verifying multiple tokens simultaneously.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive decoding in linear-attention models is inefficient due to one-token-at-a-time processing, and speculative decoding methods designed for Transformer models do not address stateful linear-attention dependencies.
AI.method: The authors developed SpecLA, a speculative decoding runtime utilizing topology-aware kernels, confidence pruning, compact factors for state recovery, and an EAGLE-style drafter to effectively handle stateful verification workflows.
AI.result: SpecLA demonstrated up to 1.70x speedup over traditional autoregressive decoding when tested with the NVIDIA H100 and the GDN-1.3B model.
AI.conclusion: SpecLA enhances the efficiency of stateful linear-attention decoding by leveraging innovative speculative verification and candidate optimization strategies.

title: DataFlow-Harness: A Grounded Code-Agent Platform for Constructing Editable LLM Data Pipelines
comment: 13 pages, 2 figures, and 5 tables. Technical report
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to automate data-processing workflows, yet coding agents typically produce scripts that are not automatically materialized as persistent, editable platform artifacts. We call this disconnect the \textit{NL2Pipeline gap}. To bridge it, we introduce \textsc{DataFlow-Harness}, a platform that guides an LLM agent to construct platform-native directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) through typed, incremental mutations rather than free-form scripts. The platform combines \textsc{DataFlow-Skills} for procedural guidance, a Model Context Protocol (MCP) layer that exposes the live operator registry and current pipeline state, and \textsc{DataFlow-WebUI}, which synchronizes conversational authoring with a visual DAG editor. On a 12-task data-engineering benchmark, \textsc{DataFlow-Harness} achieves a 93.3\% observed end-to-end pass rate. Relative to Vanilla Claude Code, it reduces measured monetary cost by 72.5\% and generation latency by 49.9\%; its observed pass rate is within 0.9 percentage points of the Context-Aware Claude Code baseline while its cost is 42.8\% lower. Per-task analysis indicates that Skills are most useful when construction depends on implicit procedural knowledge. These results show that live platform grounding can produce persistent, editable workflow artifacts with an observed reliability close to script-generation baselines and with lower measured construction cost and latency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DataFlow-Harness, a platform that guides large language models (LLMs) to create platform-native DAGs for persistent and editable workflows, achieving high reliability and reducing cost and latency.
AI.motivation: Automated workflows created by LLMs often result in scripts that are not persistent or editable within platforms, posing challenges for workflow reliability and usability.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel platform, DataFlow-Harness, which utilizes procedural guidance, a Model Context Protocol (MCP) to expose live pipeline state, and a synchronized WebUI for conversational and visual DAG editing.
AI.result: On a benchmark of 12 data-engineering tasks, DataFlow-Harness achieved a 93.3% pass rate, reduced monetary costs by 72.5%, and latency by 49.9%, delivering reliability close to script-based approaches while being more cost-efficient.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that grounding LLM-driven workflows in live platform contexts leads to persistent, reliable, and cost-effective artifact construction, outperforming traditional script-generation methods in many aspects.

title: Dropout and Random Gradient Masking Are Asymptotically Equivalent in Large ResNets
summary: Dropout and Random Gradient Masking (RaM) are two training techniques used to improve performance in deep learning. Both techniques inject randomness into the training dynamics, but in significantly different ways: dropout applies random masks to the activations in the forward pass, whereas RaM leaves the forward pass unchanged and instead masks the gradients. In particular, the noise induced by RaM in the parameter updates is unbiased, so standard explanations for the effectiveness of dropout, such as the penalization effect or the prevention of co-adaptation between neurons, do not apply to RaM. In this work, we show that the difference between the two methods disappears for ResNets in the large depth and width asymptotics: in the complete feature learning regime, they both converge to the same large-scale limiting dynamics. This asymptotic equivalence holds for several variants of dropout and RaM, including layerwise dropout as used in stochastic-depth ResNets, albeit at slower quantitative rates. In fact, we also show that several of these variants collapse to the same limit asymptotically.

AI.tldr: Dropout and Random Gradient Masking (RaM) training techniques become asymptotically equivalent for deep ResNets in large scale, despite their differing mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To understand and explore the similarities and differences in training dynamics between dropout and RaM for deep learning models, particularly ResNets.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of training dynamics to evaluate and compare the limiting behaviors of dropout and RaM for ResNets in large depth and width conditions.
AI.result: Dropout and RaM converge to the same large-scale limiting dynamics in ResNets with deep and wide architectures under complete feature learning.
AI.conclusion: Despite their differences, dropout and RaM exhibit asymptotically equivalent behaviors for deep ResNets, which unifies their understanding in the context of large-scale training.

title: Certifiable Safe Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Control-Affine Dynamics Approximation
summary: Safe model-based reinforcement learning (RL) often bridges control-theoretic analysis and RL for robots to safely explore (partially) unknown system dynamics while deriving control actions for task efficiency. The control performance and safety assurance typically rely on prior knowledge of partially modeled nominal system dynamics and the data-driven models that compensate for residual model uncertainties. However, existing methods often overlook the structure of residual model uncertainties (e.g., components affine in control), which could lead to overly conservative robot behaviors or invalid safety guarantees under the safe learning-based controllers. This paper proposes a safe reinforcement learning framework that learns control-affine dynamics with a certifiable data-driven safe policy using control barrier functions (CBF). Specifically, we first use Control-Affine Random Fourier Features (ARFF) to model robot dynamics in a control-affine form, which offers computational efficiency that scales with dataset size and reduces potential model bias for model-based reinforcement learning. Then, a model-free, efficient uncertainty quantification method using adaptive conformal prediction (ACP) is applied to quantify the uncertainty in the safety constraint arising from the learned control-affine dynamics. This allows for data-driven safety assurance amenable to principled and efficient controller synthesis with CBF. Simulation results on the cartpole and the 3D quadrotor platforms demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: A safe reinforcement learning framework is proposed to handle control-affine dynamics with certifiable safety using control barrier functions, delivering effective simulation results on robotic platforms.
AI.motivation: Existing methods fail to factor in the structure of residual model uncertainties, leading to inefficiencies in robot behavior and invalid safety assurances.
AI.method: The framework models robot dynamics using ARFF for computational efficiency and introduces ACP for principled uncertainty quantification in safety constraints. Control Barrier Functions are employed for controller synthesis.
AI.result: Simulation results on cartpole and 3D quadrotor platforms validate the framework's efficiency and safety assurance.
AI.conclusion: This methodology enables robust safe exploration and effective controller synthesis in reinforcement learning scenarios leveraging control-affine dynamics.

title: MAC 2026: Advancing Micro-Action Analysis Towards Fine-Grained Understanding
comment: Challenge Summary Paper of the 3rd Micro-Action Analysis Grand Challenge (MAC 2026) at ACM Multimedia 2026
summary: Micro-Actions (MAs) are subtle and spontaneous human behaviors that provide important non-verbal cues in social interaction and affective communication. However, their short duration, weak motion patterns, and fine-grained semantic differences make them difficult to annotate, model, and evaluate in a standardized manner. To promote academic research on micro-action analysis, we proposed and have annually organized the Micro-Action Analysis Grand Challenge (MAC) as a public benchmark platform for this emerging field. The first two editions of MAC established standardized evaluation settings for micro-action recognition and detection, providing publicly accessible datasets and protocols. Building upon these editions, this paper presents the 3rd MAC, held in conjunction with ACM Multimedia 2026. Under the theme of moving from recognition to fine-grained micro-action understanding, this edition further expands the scope of the challenge beyond conventional recognition and detection. In particular, we introduce a new task named fine-grained micro-action understanding, evaluated with the assistance of multimodal large language models, aiming to assess models' ability to capture fine-grained semantic cues and interpret subtle human micro-actions at a deeper level. We summarize the datasets, task settings, evaluation protocols, competition results, and representative solutions from top-performing teams.
