comment: 17 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables. Data from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI). Code available upon reasonable request
summary: Background. Predicting conversion from mild cognitive impairment (MCI) to Alzheimer's disease (AD) is central to trial enrichment and care planning, yet existing models provide no individual-level uncertainty estimates and rarely include transparent leakage audits. We introduce the first application of persistent homology to longitudinal clinical trajectory point clouds for this task, and the first split-conformal individual risk guarantee for any AD-conversion model. Methods. We analysed 741 MCI subjects (240 converters, 32.4%) from ADNI with a uniform 4-year follow-up cap. Five leakage sources were corrected; without them a naive pipeline achieved AUC=0.934, inflated by +0.075. Vietoris-Rips persistent homology and sublevel-set proxies were combined with trajectory slopes and engineered features (76 total) in a stacking ensemble evaluated by 5-fold cross-validation. Results. Cox and Random Survival Forest models with TDA features achieved concordance C=0.799 and C=0.826 versus C=0.753 and C=0.812 without (+0.045 and +0.014). The primary nested AUC is 0.840 (same-fold bound 0.866); external AUC was 0.879 on a zero-overlap ADNI-2/GO/3 cohort. H0 persistence entropy was the top SHAP feature and significantly associated with APOE4 dosage (Spearman r=-0.191, p<0.0001, Bonferroni-corrected). Cross-conformal coverage was 90.4%+-2.2% (target 90%); empirical external coverage 96.9%. Maximum fairness gap in false-negative rate across seven subgroups was 0.092. Conclusions. We propose H0 persistence entropy as a topological biomarker of cognitive decline and demonstrate that a leakage-audited, conformally calibrated pipeline reaches competitive accuracy with individual-level uncertainty quantification not previously available for this task.

AI.tldr: This paper aims to improve prediction models for MCI-to-AD conversion using persistent homology, leakage audits, and conformal calibration, achieving competitive accuracy with individual-level uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: The paper focuses on enhancing predictive models for MCI-to-AD conversion, aiming to enrich clinical trials and care planning while addressing existing model issues like leakage and lack of uncertainty estimates.
AI.method: The authors use Vietoris-Rips persistent homology combined with trajectory slopes and engineered features in stacking ensembles. Leakage audits and conformal calibration techniques are applied to ensure robust model performance.
AI.result: Models incorporating topological features achieved notable improvements in concordance and AUC, with competitive accuracy, individual risk quantification, and fairness assessments across subgroups.
AI.conclusion: H0 persistence entropy is proposed as a novel biomarker for cognitive decline, paired with conformally calibrated pipelines that outperform previous models while ensuring fairness and reliable uncertainty estimates.

title: BoxTwin: Learning Elastoplastic Articulated Object Dynamics from Videos
summary: Digital twins enable robots to anticipate and adapt to physical interactions, but existing models struggle with elastoplastic articulated objects (EAOs) that exhibit nonlinear elasticity, plastic yielding, and damage accumulation. We present BoxTwin, an interactive digital twin framework that learns the full dynamics of EAOs from videos. Our pipeline reconstructs the scene, identifies a physics aware constitutive model for each EAO. Experiments on manual folding and dual arm manipulation of EAOs show that BoxTwin accurately tracks joint trajectories and reproduces post contact plastic behavior over long horizons. By integrating video driven reconstruction with elastoplastic damage modeling, BoxTwin advances digital twins toward predictive, adaptive control of deformable articulated objects in unstructured environments.

AI.tldr: BoxTwin is a digital twin framework that models and tracks complex elastoplastic articulated objects (EAOs) using video data.
AI.motivation: Existing digital twin models struggle to accurately predict and interact with EAOs, which are characterized by nonlinear elasticity, plastic yielding, and damage accumulation.
AI.method: BoxTwin learns EAO dynamics from videos by reconstructing the scene and applying physics-aware constitutive models. It integrates video-driven reconstruction and elastoplastic damage modeling.
AI.result: BoxTwin demonstrates the ability to accurately track joint movements and reproduce plastic behavior over extended periods during manual folding and dual arm manipulation experiments.
AI.conclusion: BoxTwin enhances the predictive and adaptive control of deformable articulated objects, advancing digital twin capabilities in complex and unstructured environments.

title: Depth-Regularized JEPA World Models Learn More Transferable Representations from Real Outdoor Robot Data
summary: World models, especially based on JEPA architectures, have been shown to learn robust dynamics of various environments. However, learning from visually complex real-world data remains a challenge, especially in unpredictable outdoor environments. We introduce depth as a geometric prior during training in learning more robust latent dynamics directly from robot video data and handling visual complexity. This combines depth supervision with an isotropy-inducing latent regularizer (SIGReg), maximizing task-agnostic latent diversity while constraining how that diversity is organized, with the combined objective targeting the highest-entropy representation consistent with scene geometry. To satisfy this greater complexity without increasing inference time, we also add training-only overparameterization. Training an 18M-parameter model on video from a real agricultural robot, we evaluate with frozen-representation visual odometry probes, predictor-based surprise detection, and multi-step latent rollout fidelity. Compared to the baseline LeWM, our method lowers visual odometry probe error by 33%, substantially increases surprise-score separation both in-domain and on the out-of-domain TartanGround benchmark, and improves multi-step rollout fidelity under domain shift, with gains that grow with rollout horizon. Notably, we also see improvements in surprise-score separation on physics understanding that is not directly tied to 3D geometry, such as lighting and shadows. These results show that a lightweight training-time geometric prior makes a compact JEPA world model more useful and more transferable on real outdoor data with strong underlying representations, without adding inference overhead. Our work suggests that depth as a physically grounded prior can enhance world model generalization on a variety of tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes integrating depth data as a geometric prior and an isotropy-inducing latent regularizer (SIGReg) to improve latent dynamics learning in visually complex, real-world robot video data, achieving better performance on tasks like visual odometry and out-of-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Learning robust world models from visually complex real-world environments remains challenging, particularly for unpredictable outdoor settings. Existing methods struggle to incorporate coherent latent dynamics from highly variable real-world data.
AI.method: The paper introduces a method that combines depth supervision with SIGReg (an isotropy-inducing latent regularizer) to maximize task-agnostic latent diversity while maintaining scene geometry organization. It adds overparameterization during training to accommodate complexity without increasing inference time.
AI.result: Compared to a baseline (LeWM), the proposed method improves visual odometry probe accuracy by 33%, enhances surprise detection across domain shifts, and achieves superior multi-step rollout fidelity for out-of-domain scenarios. It also improves representation generalizations for non-geometry-based phenomena, such as lighting and shadows.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating depth as a geometric prior within a JEPA architecture leads to more robust, lightweight world models that generalize better to real-world outdoor data across diverse tasks, without increasing inference complexity.

title: More Than Memory: Task-Conditioned Signed FFN Writes in Long-Context Retrieval
summary:
FFNs are often treated as parametric memories. In long-context retrieval, however, the sharper question is not only what they store, but whether their native residual writes push the current retrieval state toward or away from the correct answer. We test this by scaling the model's own FFN write one layer at a time, without editing weights or injecting external steering vectors.
  Across controlled literal and semantic retrieval suites, native FFN response surfaces are signed, layer-specific, and task-conditioned: the final FFN is a suppressor in 7 of 8 model-suite cases, and 60% of layers switch role between retrieval modes (95% CI [50%, 69%]). A local directional derivative along the native write separates the two monotone roles: suppressors have negative derivative in 34/35 cases, and amplifiers have positive derivative in 18/18 cases, so the roles are not reducible to write size. On a safety-filtered LongBench retrieval-QA probe, the same diagnostic predicts attenuation damage with raw R^2=0.796 on Qwen2.5-7B and 0.791 on Qwen3.5-9B; a held-out suppressor-attenuation policy improves retrieval margins over random and norm-matched controls. These results show that native FFN scaling exposes a signed, task-conditioned residual-write structure in retrieval, and that write-gradient alignment is a compact diagnostic for the two monotone roles.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how Feedforward Networks (FFNs) in models influence retrieval tasks by scaling their native writes, uncovering patterns that inform retrieval performance and enabling diagnostics for improvement.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to explore how FFNs function as parametric memories in retrieval tasks, specifically whether their native residual writes contribute positively or negatively to obtaining the correct answer.
AI.method: The study involves scaling FFN writes layer-wise without adjusting weights or introducing external modifications, using controlled literal and semantic retrieval datasets and analyzing layer-specific responses.
AI.result: Findings reveal that FFN responses are signed, task-conditioned, and layer-specific, with suppressors and amplifiers behaving differently across cases. Predictive diagnostics based on FFN gradients improve retrieval performance on safety-filtered QA tasks.
AI.conclusion: The research highlights that FFNs exhibit a structured, task-conditioned residual-write behavior in retrieval tasks, and gradient-based diagnostics provide a tool to optimize these outputs effectively.

title: Just A Rather Very Intelligent Spoken Agent
summary: Long-horizon AI agents are becoming increasingly capable, yet their interaction with users remains surprisingly thin. In most workflows, users give an initial instruction, receive only selective textual updates, and lose a clear sense of what the agent is doing or when to step in. This leaves a missing part in the current agent ecosystem: an always-on Jarvis-style mediator that keeps the agent continuously reachable to the user. Such a mediator should support real-time spoken interaction with the user, answer questions without interrupting the worker, proactively report progress or confusion, and inject user guidance back into the agent's execution when useful. In this work, we introduce JarvisBench, a benchmark for measuring the dual value of mediation in long-horizon agent workflows. JarvisBench contains two complementary tracks: an agent-collaboration track that measures whether mediation improves downstream task completion, and a user-interaction track that measures whether mediation makes ongoing execution more understandable, responsive, and accessible to users. We instantiate the benchmark with a modular reference Jarvis prototype and evaluate it on 34 text-only WildClaw tasks executed in OpenClaw. Preliminary results with GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini-based, and GPT-based worker agents suggest that Jarvis-style mediation can provide trace-grounded responses to user questions and improve task performance when sparse user guidance is injected at appropriate moments. The results also show that effectiveness depends strongly on the mediator's LLM brain, highlighting both the promise of this missing middle layer and the need for broader community effort. Demo page https://cchen1436.github.io/jarvis

AI.tldr: JarvisBench introduces a way to evaluate the effectiveness of always-on mediators for long-horizon AI agents, enabling real-time, user-friendly interaction and task guidance.
AI.motivation: The lack of constant and accessible interaction between users and long-horizon AI agents creates inefficiency and disconnect, leaving users unable to provide guidance or stay informed about agent activities.
AI.method: The benchmark consists of two tracks—agent collaboration and user interaction—using a modular Jarvis prototype to measure dual mediation value in tasks executed on OpenClaw.
AI.result: Preliminary results show that mediation improves task execution and user understanding when supported by sparse guidance and depends on the mediator's quality (LLM brain).
AI.conclusion: Jarvis-style mediation has strong potential to bridge the gap in AI workflow responsiveness and effectiveness but requires further refinement and community involvement.

title: Team DACTYL at PAN 2026: Bayesian Data Mixing and Empirical X-risk Minimization for AI-text Detection
summary: Existing research shows that AI-generated text detection classifiers achieve strong in-distribution (ID) performance but do not maintain the same performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) texts, suggesting overfitting to dataset-specific features. However, combining different training datasets doesn't always improve performance and, in some cases, can even encourage shortcut learning. To address this issue, we fine-tune BERT-tiny models with Bayesian classification heads to select texts across three different datasets to use as a consolidated training set. We trained three different classifiers: fine-tuned DeBERTa-V3-large and ModernBERT-large classifiers via empirical X-risk minimization, and an MCGrad model that calibrates the predictions from the ModernBERT-large classifier. The DeBERTa-V3-large-large classifier achieves a mean score of 0.882 on the PAN 2026 test set across five metrics: AUROC, $F_1$, C@1, Brier score, and $F_{0.5u}$. ModernBERT-large achieves a score of 0.96 while MCGrad achieves the best score of the three with a mean score of 0.974, ranking second on the leaderboard. Our results highlight that careful dataset curation can lead to strong OOD performance. We release our ModernBERT-large and DeBERTa-V3-large models at https://huggingface.co/collections/ShantanuT01/panclef-2026 .

AI.tldr: This paper tackles the challenge of AI-generated text detection on out-of-distribution (OOD) data by fine-tuning models with Bayesian classification heads and careful dataset curation, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: AI-generated text detection classifiers excel on in-distribution (ID) data but struggle on OOD data, likely due to overfitting. This issue highlights the need for improved training approaches to achieve effective generalization.
AI.method: The authors fine-tuned BERT-tiny models with Bayesian heads to select useful data across three datasets for training. They trained three classifiers: DeBERTa-V3-large and ModernBERT-large using X-risk minimization, and MCGrad for calibrated predictions.
AI.result: DeBERTa-V3-large achieved 0.882, ModernBERT-large 0.96, and MCGrad 0.974 (second on the leaderboard) on the PAN 2026 test set using five performance metrics.
AI.conclusion: Proper dataset curation improves OOD detection significantly, and the proposed methods demonstrate strong generalization performance for AI text detection.

title: IssueExec: A Test-Driven Approach for Localizing Software Engineering Issues
summary: Issue localization, which identifies code locations requiring modification from issue descriptions, is a critical step in automated software maintenance. Existing approaches predominantly attempt to directly align issue descriptions with code elements, yet often struggle due to the inherent abstraction gap between the issue description and code implementation. Seeking alternative signals, our theoretical analysis suggests that test suites can serve as executable proxies for requirements, reducing localization uncertainty by 7.73 bits of entropy on average. A large-scale empirical study on 18 repositories validates this premise: existing tests cover 96.98\% of ground-truth files, and the two-hop pathway yields stronger semantic connectivity than direct matching in 82.4\% of cases. Despite their potential, leveraging tests for localization faces two key challenges: the semantic gap separating issue descriptions from test identifiers, and the substantial noise in execution traces from infrastructure code. To address these, we propose IssueExec, which bridges the semantic gap through domain-knowledge-enhanced test representations and filters noise via hierarchical trace analysis. Experiments on SWE-bench Lite show that IssueExec achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving function-level Recall@1 by 41.57\% over the strongest baseline. When integrated into the Agentless pipeline, IssueExec resolves 17.72\% more issues, demonstrating practical downstream benefits.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces IssueExec, a novel approach using test suites for issue localization in software maintenance, which achieves state-of-the-art results with superior recall and practical effectiveness.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve issue localization in software maintenance by reducing abstraction gaps between issue descriptions and code, leveraging test suites as proxies for requirements.
AI.method: The method called IssueExec uses domain-knowledge-enhanced test representations and hierarchical trace analysis to enhance issue localization performance.
AI.result: IssueExec surpasses existing approaches with a 41.57% improvement in function-level Recall@1 and resolves 17.72% more issues when integrated into a specific pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Test suites are effective executable proxies for requirements, and IssueExec demonstrates significant advancements for issue localization with practical downstream benefits.

title: Calibrating Semantic Uncertainty from Observable Language-Model Probabilities
summary: Language models produce probabilities over words, but professional decisions require uncertainty over meaningful states such as diagnoses, hypotheses or operational conditions. A model's printed numerical confidence does not establish reliability. We introduce a semantic map: a prespecified, testable bridge from probabilities over verbal responses to probabilities over declared states, formulated as semiparametric inference for a finite-valued latent state. A reference model defines the target posterior, the language model supplies an unrestricted conditional distribution over verbal responses, and held-out calibration connects them. We derive posterior-error bounds and conditions for existence, uniqueness, stability and sequential Bayesian updating. Crucially, language probabilities depend on the prompt's lexical form, whereas the target posterior is unchanged by information-equivalent rewording. We test the method on professional market text compiled from Federal Reserve economic and financial series and on controlled simulations with exact posteriors. Across two fitted language models, language-derived probabilities outperform printed numerical probabilities, recover held-out posteriors with valid uncertainty coverage, remain largely stable under paraphrasing, and respond appropriately to altered evidence. The broader implication is that prompt engineering optimizes a wording-dependent response, whereas scientific and professional use requires validated stability of application-relevant meaning. The semantic map turns this general concern into a testable statistical problem and, when its acceptance conditions hold, yields an auditable posterior estimate. The same principle offers a template for auditing classifications, recommendations and other fluent responses that may conceal semantic instability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a semantic map to bridge language model probabilities with valid probabilities over professional decision-relevant states, ensuring stability under rewording and enabling reliable uncertainty estimations.
AI.motivation: Language models lack robust and reliable mechanisms for providing uncertainty estimates over decision-critical states like diagnoses or operational conditions.
AI.method: A semantic map is introduced, utilizing semiparametric inference to align language model outputs with target posteriors, supported by posterior-error bounds, calibration, and robustness testing under lexical changes.
AI.result: Language-derived probabilities outperform standard printed numerical probabilities, demonstrate valid uncertainty coverage, remain stable under rewording, and effectively respond to altered evidence.
AI.conclusion: The semantic map provides a systematic and testable approach to ensure semantic stability in applications of language models, addressing critical issues in professional and scientific use cases.

title: VIDAR: Visual-Inertial Dense Alignment and Reconstruction via a Geometric Foundation Model
summary: Monocular foundation models provide dense geometry but usually lack a stable metric scale. This paper presents VIDAR, a visual-inertial dense reconstruction framework that couples SVO+IMU odometry with Depth Anything 3. VIDAR uses the visual-inertial front end as a metric anchor: it provides camera poses, scale, and a consistent world frame for aligning dense foundation-model predictions across time. The foundation model then contributes detailed local geometry that is fused into a global reconstruction. We study both pose-conditioned DA3 and a decoupled alignment strategy. On EuRoC, pose injection reduces scale error to about 1\% and reaches 0.463 mean F@0.10; the decoupled hybrid improves this to 0.676 without ground-truth poses. Results on EuRoC and TUM RGB-D show that VIDAR is a practical route to metric dense monocular reconstruction.

AI.tldr: VIDAR is a framework that combines visual-inertial odometry and a foundation model for improved dense monocular reconstruction with metric scale.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of stable metric scale in monocular foundation models used for dense reconstruction by coupling them with visual-inertial odometry.
AI.method: The framework uses SVO+IMU odometry as a metric anchor to provide scale, poses, and a consistent world frame, and integrates Depth Anything 3 for detailed local geometry.
AI.result: VIDAR achieves significant improvements, reducing scale errors to about 1% and achieving 0.676 mean F@0.10 without using ground-truth poses on test datasets like EuRoC and TUM RGB-D.
AI.conclusion: VIDAR demonstrates a practical and effective approach for achieving metric dense monocular reconstruction by combining visual-inertial odometry and foundation models.

title: Eddy-VL 1.9B: Structural Pruning and Layered Distillation for Edge-Deployable Multimodal Embedding
comment: 11pages,4figures,Model weights and inference code are available on Hugging Face
summary: In this report, we introduce Eddy-VL 1.9B, a compressed multimodal embedding model built on Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B for offline, edge-deployable vision-language retrieval. Eddy-VL targets air-gapped forensic and investigative settings where cloud APIs are unavailable and low latency is essential. Compression combines (i) probe-driven structural pruning that removes four redundant text-decoder layers (28 to 24) ranked by adjacent-layer linear CKA, and (ii) layered knowledge distillation with hole-covering teacher-student mappings, mid-layer attention-map 1-CKA, and final-layer MSE and cosine losses with Matryoshka dimensions {128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048}. The released model contains 1,926,188,032 parameters (3.85 GB bf16), representing approximately 9.5% fewer parameters than the 2.13B teacher model. Empirical evaluations on MMEB-V2 (78 tasks, VLM2Vec protocol) show that Eddy-VL achieves an overall score of 63.2 compared with 68.9 for the teacher, retaining 91.7% of the teacher's performance while recovering 6.4 of the 12.1 points lost through pruning alone (56.8). Compositional reasoning performance remains close to the teacher on SugarCrepe (86.1 vs. 86.4), MR2-Bench (24.5 vs. 24.7), and ARO (59.5 vs. 60.4), while Winoground group performance (6.8 vs. 8.5) remains the primary limitation. Depth pruning also reduces forward latency by approximately 10% (150.0 to 136.4 ms per image on NVIDIA DGX Spark using FlashAttention-2). We present the architecture, compression methodology, training procedures, and evaluation results, demonstrating the effectiveness of Eddy-VL for multimodal retrieval under constrained edge deployment. Model weights and inference code are publicly available on Hugging Face.

AI.tldr: Eddy-VL 1.9B is a compressed vision-language retrieval model tailored for offline, edge-based use. Retains 91.7% of teacher model's performance while being 9.5% smaller and faster.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to develop a compact, efficient vision-language model suitable for air-gapped, low-latency environments where cloud-based solutions are not an option.
AI.method: The approach involves structural pruning of text-decoder layers based on linear CKA analysis and layered knowledge distillation using advanced teacher-student techniques, including attention-map and dimension-aware loss functions.
AI.result: Eddy-VL has 9.5% fewer parameters than its teacher model, achieves 63.2 overall score (91.7% retention of teacher's performance), and reduces latency by ~10% while closely maintaining the teacher's performance on key benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Eddy-VL demonstrates that targeted compression techniques can produce a model effective for edge applications without significant performance loss. It achieves efficiency and latency improvements necessary for practical deployment on constrained systems.

title: Feature Generation Using LLMs: An Evolutionary Algorithm Approach
summary: A crucial step in machine learning pipelines is to present each entity with features or attributes that are representative of the characteristics of the processed entities. Feature engineering is an important step in finding a relation among attributes that otherwise may not be processed by the ML algorithms. Meanwhile, Large Language Models have shown promising abilities in coding, mathematical reasoning, and processing world knowledge. In this work, we utilize an LLM for the problem of feature generation from tabular data based on the previously given features. We have created a pipeline that takes a set of attributes and a prompt to generate new features. Then, our selection algorithm selects the best-performing sets of attributes. We apply our method to eight datasets from different domains and data types. Our results show that, in most cases, the language model can produce new features based on mathematical and logical operators that are useful for the given tasks and can improve classification results.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using large language models (LLMs) to generate new features from tabular data to enhance machine learning classification performance.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of identifying meaningful relationships among attributes in tabular data, leveraging the capabilities of LLMs for automated feature generation.
AI.method: They propose a pipeline where LLMs generate new features based on a set of attributes and prompts, followed by a selection algorithm to identify the best-performing features.
AI.result: The developed approach was tested on eight datasets across different domains, showing that LLM-generated features could improve classification performance in most scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Utilizing LLMs for feature engineering in tabular data appears promising, as their produced features often enhance ML algorithm performance.

title: A Research Prototype for Closed-Loop Generative Design of Customized Foot Orthoses via Semantic-Physics Alignment
summary: Translating unstructured clinical prescriptions into patient-specific foot orthoses (FOs) is hindered by a semantic-physical misalignment: high-level clinical intent is not mapped deterministically onto the 3D geometric parameters of the orthosis, and existing design workflows remain dependent on manual expertise with no instantaneous biomechanical validation. We present TANS-FO, a research prototype-a modular pipeline with closed-loop feedback for computational design automation of customized FOs, not a clinically validated therapeutic device. A Text-Aligned Neural Surrogate (TANS) uses cross-attention to project clinical-text embeddings onto a continuous lattice-density field, while a Graph Neural Network (GNN) surrogate predicts plantar stress in real time as a substitute for Finite Element Analysis (FEA). The framework is anchored on the open-access PicoFoot-5K anthropometric database (5,230 subjects; 30+ anatomical parameters). Under standardized quasi-static loading, the GNN surrogate agrees with an Abaqus reference solver (R^2 = 0.94), and the full pipeline synthesizes manufacturing-ready lattice insoles within minutes. On the Male 18-40 cohort, the proposed system attains a surrogate-predicted peak-pressure reduction of 34.7% over parametric CAD, with a fit error of 0.42 mm. Separately, an exploratory feasibility observation (n = 12; 2-week follow-up; no control group) using VAS pain reporting indicates short-term comfort improvement (VAS 6.4 -> 2.1), but this data is explicitly classified as preliminary observational evidence only-not evidence of clinical efficacy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TANS-FO, an automated computational framework for designing patient-specific foot orthoses (FOs) using neural networks for biomechanical validation.
AI.motivation: Current workflows for designing foot orthoses rely heavily on manual expertise and lack automated methods for mapping clinical intent to 3D geometric designs with biomechanical validation.
AI.method: The framework incorporates a Text-Aligned Neural Surrogate (TANS) for projecting clinical text into geometric designs and a Graph Neural Network (GNN) surrogate for real-time plantar stress prediction. Both are anchored on the PicoFoot-5K database.
AI.result: The model achieved high agreement (R^2 = 0.94) with an Abaqus solver for stress prediction, synthesized FOs within minutes, reduced peak pressure by 34.7%, and minimized fit error to 0.42 mm. Preliminary feasibility data indicated comfort improvement based on VAS pain scores.
AI.conclusion: TANS-FO can automate and validate FO design but is a research prototype with limited clinical validation to date.

title: The Librarian Who Refused to Code: Model-Dependent Identity Enactment in LLM Code Generation
comment: 17 pages, 3 tables, no figures. Ancillary files include raw completions, derived scores, analysis scripts, persona texts, and preregistration
summary: Biographical personas are widely used in system prompts, but their effects on code generation are rarely evaluated under controlled, pre-registered conditions. We tested four prompt conditions (no persona, two engineer personas, and a research-librarian persona), 12 code-generation tasks, two frontier models, and five runs per cell (480 completions). Persona effects differed between the two tested models. Under the pre-registered mixed-effects analysis, the condition-by-model interaction was significant for provider-reported output tokens; a post-hoc visible-character measure showed the same qualitative pattern. Six GPT-5.5 completions were length-capped and are reported separately. On Claude Opus, the minimalist engineer persona reduced visible output by 30% (33% in provider tokens) without improving correctness, while the thorough engineer persona increased output without a correctness gain. In an exploratory post-hoc analysis, the librarian persona elicited in-character disclaimers in 55 of 60 Opus responses and 12 genuine no-code responses, lowering mean correctness from 0.92 to 0.67. GPT-5.5 produced neither behavior in its 59 non-truncated responses. These results are consistent with personas acting as Model-Dependent behavioral-policy biases rather than universal quality interventions. We release raw completions, derived scores, analysis artifacts, a pre-registration document, and an execution gate log; end-to-end test-based rescoring requires an unreleased task harness.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how different personas in prompts affect code generation across two advanced language models under controlled settings, revealing that persona effects are model-dependent and do not consistently enhance correctness.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether and how biographical personas in system prompts influence the quality and correctness of code generation, as this impact has not been previously well-studied or rigorously tested.
AI.method: The study tested four different prompt conditions (no persona, two engineer personas, and a research-librarian persona) across 12 coding tasks, two large language models (Claude Opus and GPT-5.5), with multiple runs per setup. Both pre-registered mixed-effects analysis and exploratory post-hoc analyses were conducted.
AI.result: Findings revealed that personas affect output differently depending on the model. For instance, engineer personas altered the length of output without improving correctness in Claude Opus, while librarian personas introduced behavioral disclaimers drastically lowering correctness. GPT-5.5 showed no similar behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Personas are not universal tools for improving code generation quality; instead, their impact varies between models, operating more as behavioral tweaks than quality refinements.

title: Program Synthesis for Simulation-Based Inference: Joint Model Selection and Parameter Estimation
summary: Neural simulation-based inference enables parameter estimation for complex models, but typically requires the user to specify a simulator encoding a fixed model structure. We present a framework for joint model selection and parameter estimation that combines large language models for program synthesis with neural simulation-based inference. Given a natural language description of the system and data under investigation, an LLM proposes candidate simulator programs which are iteratively refined via feedback-driven mutation and evaluated using neural density estimation. The approach enables simulation-based inference over a pool of models, not just parameters within a fixed model. On benchmarks spanning deterministic dynamics, stochastic epidemic models, and dark matter substructure inference from gravitational-lensing images, the method identifies plausible model families from open-ended prompts, with accuracy that reflects the information content of the data and identifiability of candidate models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework combining large language models (LLMs) with neural simulation-based inference for joint model selection and parameter estimation.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation of existing neural simulation-based inference methods that rely on fixed model structures specified by the user.
AI.method: A two-step approach where LLMs propose simulator programs based on natural language descriptions, which are refined iteratively with feedback, and evaluated using neural density estimation.
AI.result: The method successfully identifies plausible model families across tasks like deterministic dynamics and gravitational-lensing image analysis, with performance tied to data informativeness and model identifiability.
AI.conclusion: This novel approach extends simulation-based inference capabilities beyond fixed models, enabling broader applicability across scientific domains.

title: Retriever: Composing Closed-Loop Asynchronous Robot Programs
comment: Project website: http://retriever.systems; Package open-source website: http://openretriever.org
summary: Building long-horizon robot agents requires composing closed-loop pipelines -- perception, belief update, planning, and control -- whose components run at different clocks and with variable latency. Today, these systems are often assembled with ad-hoc concurrency and pub/sub conventions that make timing and input-consumption semantics implicit, yielding schedule-dependent behavior that is hard to reproduce, debug, and reuse. Current solutions typically solve parts of this problem at either the algorithmic or the systems layer, but not both. In this work, we propose Retriever, which spans the entire stack: an asynchronous decision model, a programming model, a runtime, and an example closed-loop agent pipeline. Retriever represents an agent as a graph of stateful causal stream functions executed on explicit run clocks. We formalize this view via an asynchronous environment-agent loop over continuous-time streams and show that finite-memory causal policies can be represented by compositions of these operators. Retriever compiles these graphs into a runtime that supports multiple backends, enabling systematic debugging across running environments and deterministic replay from logged asynchronous data. We evaluate Retriever through a real-robot case study together with controlled studies of runtime overhead and deterministic replay behavior.

AI.tldr: Retriever provides a unified approach to building robust long-horizon robot agents with determinism and systematic debugging, addressing timing and semantic challenges.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the challenge of building robot agents with reliable timing and behavior across components like perception, belief, planning, and control, which often operate asynchronously but lack reproducibility and debugging support.
AI.method: The authors introduced Retriever, an architecture encompassing an asynchronous decision model, programming model, runtime, and pipeline representation for agents relying on explicit clocks and stateful causal stream functions.
AI.result: Retriever enables deterministic replay and systematic debugging of robot pipelines while showcasing its efficacy through real-world and controlled studies, analyzing runtime overhead and replay robustness.
AI.conclusion: Retriever enhances the reproducibility, debugging capabilities, and systematic composability of heterogeneous components in robot agent pipelines, spanning both algorithmic and systems-level challenges.

title: Monte Carlo Dropout Uncertainty and Entropy-Thresholded Selective Prediction for Architecture-Agnostic Brain Tumor MRI Triage
summary: Deep networks now subtype brain tumors on MRI about as well as specialist readers, yet accuracy is not what keeps them out of the clinic. What matters at the point of care is whether a model's confidence can be trusted to flag the cases it is likely to misclassify and defer them to a human. Deterministic estimates cannot: an auxiliary confidence head trained alongside the classifier collapses to a near-constant output that says nothing about correctness. This study proposes an uncertainty-first pipeline for four-class brain tumor MRI (glioma, meningioma, pituitary, no tumor) that reads predictive uncertainty from Monte Carlo (MC) Dropout over T = 20 passes and turns the resulting entropy into an explicit rule for deferring uncertain cases to a radiologist. We partitioned 7,200 images by perceptual-hash cluster, closing the near-duplicate leakage that inflates accuracy under naive splitting, and evaluated the pipeline on ViT-B/16 and ResNet-50 across five seeds along three axes: discrimination, calibration, and selective prediction. Both discriminate strongly (macro-AUC 0.994; accuracy 0.962 and 0.964), and no seed separates them (0 of 5 significant, p < 0.05), so the result is driven by the uncertainty pipeline, not the network. A single temperature scalar pulls the deterministic softmax into tight calibration (expected calibration error 0.016-0.020), and deferring the most uncertain 5% of cases lifts accuracy on the rest to about 0.98 on both (area under the risk-coverage curve 0.010-0.011). MC-Dropout uncertainty here is thus calibrated, non-collapsing, and directly actionable through a concrete deferral rule, providing an architecture-agnostic basis for calibrated, defer-to-human brain tumor MRI triage under internal validation.

AI.tldr: This study develops an architecture-agnostic pipeline for brain tumor MRI classification that integrates uncertainty estimation to defer potentially misclassified cases to radiologists, improving reliability for clinical use.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning models excel at brain tumor subtyping but lack trustworthy confidence measures to identify uncertain classifications for deferral to specialists. This gap limits their clinical deployment.
AI.method: The study introduces an uncertainty-first approach using MC Dropout (T=20 passes) to calculate predictive uncertainty. An explicit entropy-based deferral rule sends uncertain cases to radiologists. Data splitting resolves near-duplicate leakages by perceptual-hash clustering.
AI.result: The pipeline achieves high accuracy (~0.964), robust calibration (ECE 0.016-0.020), and effective uncertainty-guided deferral. Deferring 5% of the most uncertain cases improves accuracy on the remaining set to ~0.98, showing consistent selectivity across five seeds.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides reliable confidence estimates and actionable deferral rules for medical imaging, making it suitable for clinical settings by ensuring safe and accurate triage of brain tumor MRI cases.

title: TopoTuner: Topological Finetuning of Large Language Models
summary:
Full fine-tuning remains a strong way to adapt pretrained LLMs, but it updates all weights and can be expensive. LoRA reduces the number of trainable parameters, but it does not directly answer which pretrained components should be trained and which can be frozen during adaptation. We introduce TopoTuner, a topology-guided fine-tuning framework for selective freezing of attention projection matrices. \method treats each projection matrix as a row cloud and uses Wasserstein distances between persistence diagrams to measure how its topology changes during fine-tuning.
  TopoTuner learns a reusable freezing profile from a source dataset and transfers it to efficiently fine-tune models on out-of-domain datasets, evaluating whether task-specific topological drift generalizes across question answering and sentiment analysis tasks.
  Across LLaMA-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, and Qwen3-8B-Base, TopoTuner is competitive with full fine-tuning while training only 1-2\% of the model parameters, and outperforms LoRA in 7 out of 9 model-dataset settings, which can change up to 39.57\% of the projection parameters. Along with minimized updates, TopoTuner reduces training time by 20.4\% relative to full fine-tuning and 5.5\% relative to LoRA on average. TopoTuner opens a new direction for reusable freezing profiles, where fine-tuning behavior learned on one dataset can be shared across multiple tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TopoTuner, a selective fine-tuning framework for large language models (LLMs) using topology-guided freezing of parameters to achieve efficient performance comparable to full fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency and high computational cost of full fine-tuning for pretrained LLMs by exploring methods to selectively freeze parameters while maintaining strong fine-tuning performance.
AI.method: The framework treats attention projection matrices as row clouds, measures topological changes using Wasserstein distances between persistence diagrams, and learns reusable freezing profiles from one dataset that can be transferred to others.
AI.result: TopoTuner achieves competitive performance with full fine-tuning while training only 1-2% of model parameters. It outperforms LoRA in most tested settings, with reduced training time.
AI.conclusion: TopoTuner shows promise in creating efficient, transferable fine-tuning methods for LLMs, opening possibilities for broader application of reusable freezing profiles across tasks.

title: How Reliable Are Multimodal Signals of Conversational State? Evidence from Remote Dyadic Collaborative Tasks
comment: Accepted, to appear in Proceedings of ACM International Conference on Multimodal Interaction 2026, 13 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables
summary: Measuring conversational states such as cognitive load and conversational power from multimodal behavior requires characteristic features that are not only predictive but also reliable across task contexts. We present a three-dimensional evaluation framework assessing predictive accuracy, cross-task generalizability, and test-retest reliability, applied to interactional, acoustic, and linguistic features extracted from dyadic conversations during collaborative tasks performed over a video-conferencing platform (AVCAffe dataset; 53 dyads, 9 tasks). Our results show that no single feature family dominates all three dimensions. Linguistic features show the highest predictive accuracy for cognitive load but collapse under cross-task evaluation, revealing sensitivity to task-specific vocabulary. Additionally, acoustic reliability, often reported as evidence of feature stability, degrades once speaker identity is controlled, confirming that standard prosodic features measure vocal characteristics rather than conversational state. Interaction features provide the only genuinely reliable signal, unchanged after speaker normalization. Interestingly, classifying power role remained near chance baseline across all conditions, indicating limitations of task-level aggregated behavior for predicting power role in conversation. Our findings reveal three insights: (1) linguistic features predict best but generalize poorly across task contexts; (2) acoustic reliability collapses to near-zero once speaker identity is controlled, challenging standard evaluation practice; and (3) interaction features provide the only genuinely reliable signal, with floor dominance predicting within-dyad cognitive load asymmetry. These results argue for speaker normalization and multi-dimensional evaluation as prerequisites for context-aware, robust multimodal feature selection in conversational systems.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates multimodal features (interactional, acoustic, linguistic) for determining conversational states (e.g., cognitive load, power) using a three-dimensional framework (predictive accuracy, cross-task generalizability, test-retest reliability).
AI.motivation: To identify characteristic features that are reliable and predictive across diverse task contexts for conversational systems.
AI.method: A three-dimensional evaluation framework applied to multimodal features extracted from dyadic conversations during collaborative tasks (AVCAffe dataset).
AI.result: Linguistic features show high predictive accuracy for cognitive load but fail to generalize across tasks. Acoustic feature reliability diminishes with speaker normalization. Interaction features are most reliable. Prediction of power roles remained near baseline.
AI.conclusion: Speaker normalization and multi-dimensional evaluation are essential for robust, context-aware multimodal feature selection in conversational systems. Interaction features emerge as the most reliable signal for conversational states.

title: Combining model checking with simulation-based techniques for protocol verification
summary: Model checking is a powerful technique for verifying properties of systems or protocols, but it often suffers from the state space explosion problem. For protocols such as the Alternating Bit Protocol (ABP) and the Sliding Window Protocol (SWP), increasing parameters such as channel capacities or window sizes leads to a reachable state space that is infeasible for exhaustive model checking. To address this, we propose a technique that combines model checking with simulation relations. We focus on three protocols: the Simple Communication Protocol (SCP), ABP, and SWP. Formalized as state machines, these protocols are presented in decreasing order of abstraction: SCP, ABP, and then SWP, which results in a corresponding increase in reachable states. While SCP is verifiable through direct model checking without state space explosion, the direct application of model checking to ABP and SWP becomes infeasible as their parameter values increase. We demonstrate that ABP satisfies its invariant property by combining model checking on SCP with a simulation relation from ABP to SCP. Furthermore, we demonstrate that SWP satisfies its invariant property through the composition of simulation relations from SWP to ABP and from ABP to SCP, in conjunction with model checking on SCP. These approaches enable the formal verification of protocols with large reachable state spaces by conducting model checking on a significantly smaller state machine.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles state space explosion in model checking protocols (SCP, ABP, SWP) by combining simulation relations with model checking, enabling verification of protocols with large state spaces via smaller, abstract state machines.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the state space explosion problem in verifying protocol properties using model checking, especially for complex protocols (ABP and SWP) with increasing parameters like channel capacity and window size.
AI.method: It employs a combination of simulation relations and model checking, leveraging abstraction levels among Simple Communication Protocol (SCP), Alternating Bit Protocol (ABP), and Sliding Window Protocol (SWP). SCP is verified directly, while simulation relations connect SCP to ABP and SWP for formal verification.
AI.result: The study shows that ABP satisfies its invariant via model checking SCP combined with simulation relations. Similarly, SWP satisfies its invariant through layered simulation relations involving SCP and ABP. This allows formal verification despite large state spaces.
AI.conclusion: Combining model checking with simulation relations enables effective verification of protocols that suffer from state space explosion, making it feasible to validate complex protocols by reducing the problem to smaller state machines.

title: Optimizing the Preconditioner: A Black-box Online-to-Nonconvex Conversion with Static Regret Minimization Oracles
summary:
We study whether stochastic nonconvex optimization can be reduced to ordinary static regret minimization in online convex optimization in a black-box manner. For smooth nonconvex objectives, our reduction maintains a predictable gradient tracker, while a black-box online learner selects a preconditioner that determines how this tracker is transformed into the update direction. The learner receives linear convex losses and is evaluated against a single fixed comparator over one undiscounted online game. For a $β$-smooth objective with range bounded by $M$ and an unbiased stochastic-gradient oracle with variance bounded by \(σ^2\), we establish $$\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T
  \mathbb E\!\left[\|\nabla f(x_t)\|_2^2\right]
  \lesssim
  \frac{σ\sqrt{Mβ}}{\sqrt T}
  +
  \frac{\sqrt{Mβ}\,
  \mathscr R_T(\mathcal A,I_d)}{T}
  +
  \frac{Mβ}{T}.$$ Consequently, any black-box OCO algorithm with $\mathscr R_T(\mathcal A,I_d)=O(\sqrt T)$ recovers the classical $O(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}})$ convergence rate.
  We further show that the same black-box framework extends beyond the smooth setting to Lipschitz nonconvex objectives without Lipschitz continuous gradients. Importantly, this extension continues to rely only on an ordinary static-regret guarantee and requires no stronger notion of online regret. When the OCO oracle admits square-root static regret, the resulting conversion achieves the optimal $O(T^{-2/7})$ convergence rate for the corresponding Goldstein stationary point. These results resolve the open problem posed by Chen and Hazan (2024). More broadly, our framework separates optimizer design into gradient prediction and online preconditioner selection, providing a principled perspective on how adaptive optimization methods such as AdaGrad and Shampoo may be understood through static regret and applied in nonconvex optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a reduction technique to solve stochastic nonconvex optimization using static regret in online convex optimization, achieving optimal convergence rates. The framework generalizes to both smooth and nonsmooth nonconvex settings.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address whether stochastic nonconvex optimization can be treated as a problem of static regret minimization using online convex optimization tools, thus simplifying and unifying optimization approaches.
AI.method: The paper utilizes a reduction framework where a predictable gradient tracker is combined with a black-box online learner selecting preconditioners. This approach handles both smooth and nonsmooth nonconvex objectives and relies on static regret guarantees.
AI.result: The method achieves convergence rates of $O(T^{-1/2})$ for smooth nonconvex objectives and $O(T^{-2/7})$ for Lipschitz nonconvex objectives using OCO oracles with square-root static regret.
AI.conclusion: The paper resolves an open problem by unifying nonconvex optimization under a static regret framework, offering a new perspective on adaptive methods like AdaGrad and Shampoo while achieving optimal convergence rates for a broad class of objectives.

title: Asynchronous Multimodal Diffusion Policy Composition via Latency-Aware Guidance Fusion
summary: Diffusion policies have shown strong potential for robotic imitation learning, and recent extensions incorporate additional modalities to improve manipulation performance. However, these modalities often differ not only in information content but also in sensing rates and inference latencies. Existing multimodal diffusion policies typically rely on synchronous fusion or manually designed multi-frequency architectures, which either slow down high-frequency feedback or limit extensibility to new modality combinations. We propose LAG-Fusion, a latency-aware guidance fusion framework for asynchronous multimodal diffusion policy composition. LAG-Fusion allows modality-specific policies to operate at their native inference rates and contribute denoising guidance whenever available. To make asynchronous composition consistent, we derive a reference-frame rebasing rule for diffusion variables under relative action representations, enabling delayed guidance to be aligned before fusion. We instantiate LAG-Fusion in contact-rich manipulation by composing a low-frequency vision policy with a high-frequency force policy. Experiments under heterogeneous modality latencies show that LAG-Fusion improves policy responsiveness and task performance over synchronous fusion and specially designed force-aware baselines.

AI.tldr: LAG-Fusion is a framework for asynchronous multimodal diffusion policies in robotic imitation learning, addressing latency mismatches among modalities to improve manipulation performance.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal diffusion policies struggle with incorporating modalities that differ in sensing rates and inference latencies. There is a need for a framework that balances high-frequency feedback with extensibility for new modality combinations while maintaining smooth policy operation.
AI.method: LAG-Fusion introduces a latency-aware guidance system allowing individual modality policies to operate asynchronously while contributing denoising guidance at their own native rates. It includes a reference-frame rebasing rule to align delayed guidance within a diffusion framework.
AI.result: In experiments with modalities like vision and force sensing, LAG-Fusion demonstrated superior policy responsiveness and task performance compared to synchronous fusion methods and force-aware baselines.
AI.conclusion: LAG-Fusion offers a novel solution to the challenges of asynchronous multimodal policy composition, enhancing task performance in contact-rich manipulations while maintaining modular adaptability for various modalities.

title: Deep Learning-based Filtering for Video Coding: A Survey on Architectures, Algorithms, and Complexity Analysis
summary: As Ultra-High-Definition (UHD) displays and immersive media services become ubiquitous in the Internet of Things (IoT) and Consumer Electronics (CE) sectors, including 8K display and mobile devices, the demand for high-efficiency video coding is unprecedented. While Deep Learning-based Filtering (DLF) has emerged as a promising solution to mitigate compression artifacts inherent in standards like High Efficiency Video Coding (HEVC/H.265) and Versatile Video Coding (VVC/H.266), its deployment in CE devices is severely constrained by computational complexity, memory bandwidth, and power consumption. To bridge the gap between academic research and practical deployment, this paper presents a comprehensive, hardware-oriented survey of DLF techniques. We propose a systematic three-dimensional taxonomy classifying methods into (1) Integration Scheme within the Video Coding, (2) Coding Information Utilization, and (3) Network Design Strategy. Unlike prior reviews, this work critically analyzes the trade-offs between Rate-Distortion (RD) performance and hardware feasibility, highlighting the evolution from heavy, performance-oriented models to lightweight, hardware-friendly architectures targeting Neural Processing Units (NPUs). Furthermore, we incorporate the latest standardization activities from the Joint Video Experts Team (JVET) on Neural Network-based Video Coding (NNVC) to provide realistic guidelines. We also identify open challenges such as real-time inference latency and error propagation, providing a roadmap toward robust, low-power intelligent video coding in next-generation CE vision endpoints.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews Deep Learning-based Filtering (DLF) methods for video coding and presents a hardware-oriented taxonomy. It emphasizes bridging academic research and practical deployment for efficiency in UHD displays and immersive media.
AI.motivation: The increasing demand for ultra-high-definition displays and immersive media drives the need for efficient video coding. However, deploying deep learning techniques in consumer electronics faces challenges like computational complexity and power consumption.
AI.method: The authors propose a three-dimensional taxonomy for DLF methods based on integration schemes, coding information utilization, and network design strategies. They analyzed these methods for Rate-Distortion performance and hardware feasibility.
AI.result: The study shows a shift towards lightweight, hardware-efficient DLF architectures for Neural Processing Units (NPUs) and summarizes advancements in NNVC standardization by JVET.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides guidelines and identifies challenges, such as inference latency and error propagation, for developing feasible DLF solutions for low-power, intelligent video coding in next-generation consumer devices.

title: From Outcomes to Actions: Leveraging Hindsight for Long-Horizon Language Agent Training
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a widely adopted technique for improving large language models (LLMs) on complex tasks. Despite this progress, existing RL methods still face challenges in training agents with longer-horizon interactions. One major bottleneck is distinguishing the contribution of different actions in long-horizon interaction, leading to high optimization variance. To address this, we introduce a novel policy gradient method, Hindsight Policy Optimization (HPO), that projects both the current policy distribution and the hindsight distribution into an intent space and extracts low-variance learning signals from the Wasserstein distance between them. We theoretically and empirically show that aggregating semantically similar states and actions in the intent space yields a bounded-variance estimator and improves policy performance stably. Our code is available online.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Hindsight Policy Optimization (HPO), a novel reinforcement learning method that reduces training variance for long-horizon tasks using intent space and Wasserstein distance.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the challenge in reinforcement learning of handling high optimization variance in long-horizon interactions due to difficulties in attributing the impacts of specific actions.
AI.method: The method involves projecting policy distributions into an intent space and calculating the Wasserstein distance between the current and hindsight distributions to generate low-variance learning signals.
AI.result: The approach theoretically and empirically demonstrates that grouping similar states and actions in intent space creates a bounded-variance estimator, which improves policy performance stably.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HPO method addresses optimization variance issues in RL, providing a more stable and improved policy performance for long-horizon tasks.

title: Diversity-Oriented Fine-Tuning for Uncertainty-Based Hallucination Detection
summary: Existing hallucination detection methods are typically conducted at the inference stage, without making any modifications to the model itself. In this paper, we are interested in exploring fine-tuning strategies that enhance the detectability of hallucinations in the resulting model. Focusing on semantic-entropy-based detection, we observe that many erroneous outputs remain undetected because the model produces nearly identical incorrect answers across multiple runs. To address this, we propose diversity-oriented fine-tuning to encourage more varied generations. We introduce two specific strategies: one based on Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and the other on Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Extensive experiments are conducted to evaluate our approach and analyze the behavior of the models before and after fine-tuning. We find that after adopting our fine-tuning methods, the models become less likely to produce low semantic entropy responses for hallucinated answers, thereby improving the effectiveness of hallucination detection, eventually yielding results better than or comparable with state of the art methods. The code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes diversity-oriented fine-tuning strategies to make models better at detecting hallucinations by encouraging more varied outputs and improving semantic-entropy-based detection.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination detection methods are conducted post-inference without altering the model. The authors aim to improve detection capability by adjusting the fine-tuning strategies of the models themselves.
AI.method: The authors introduce diversity-oriented fine-tuning, using two strategies: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), to increase the variation in model output and improve semantic entropy for hallucination detection.
AI.result: The models fine-tuned with the proposed methods produce fewer low semantic-entropy outputs for hallucinated answers, enhancing detection and achieving performance comparable to or better than current state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Diversity-enhancing fine-tuning strategies improve hallucination detection by making models less prone to generating uniform incorrect outputs, thus enhancing semantic-entropy-based methods.

title: Multilingual Sentence Embeddings for Linguistic-Integrated Reliability Audit
summary: Multilingual assessment systems commonly rely on translation for scoring and quality-control processes. We evaluate whether multilingual sentence embeddings can replace translated English input for Linguistic-Integrated Reliability Auditing (LiRA) across 11 PIRLS constructed-response items and three embedding models. Native-language embeddings reproduced translation-based reliability estimates closely while recovering responses excluded after translation failure, with no meaningful change in reliability.

AI.tldr: The study examines the use of multilingual sentence embeddings to avoid translation for reliability auditing in multilingual assessment systems.
AI.motivation: Translation issues in multilingual assessment systems can lead to data loss and reliability concerns. The paper aims to evaluate whether multilingual sentence embeddings can be used as an alternative to translations for reliability auditing.
AI.method: The authors tested three embedding models on 11 PIRLS constructed-response items to compare native-language embeddings against translation-based reliability estimates.
AI.result: Native-language embeddings closely matched the reliability estimates derived from translations. They also retrieved responses excluded due to translation failures, without affecting reliability.
AI.conclusion: Multilingual sentence embeddings are a viable alternative to translations for reliability auditing in assessment systems, mitigating translation-related issues.

title: PSASpotter: A Tool to Detect the Usage of Platform-Specific APIs in Python
summary: A platform-specific API is implemented for a particular platform (e.g., operating system), thus, it may not work on other platforms than the target one. Detecting the usage of such APIs is important for supporting software maintenance, as it allows maintainers to be alerted about APIs that could pose potential risks. This paper proposes PSASpotter, a tool to detect the usage of platform-specific APIs in Python systems. PSASpotter also identifies whether the platform-specific APIs are used within a defensive code, such as try/except blocks or if blocks that check the current platform. PSASpotter can support the development of novel empirical studies about the usage of platform-specific APIs in the Python ecosystem. Moreover, the defensive code detected by PSASpotter may contain alternative solutions for unavailable APIs, which can provide insights for software development and testing across multiple platforms. PSASpotter is available at: https://github.com/ricardojob/PSASpotter. Tool video: https://youtu.be/d3WyozTAKS8.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PSASpotter, a tool to detect platform-specific API usage in Python, including whether such APIs are protected by defensive code.
AI.motivation: Platform-specific APIs can hinder software portability and pose risks, making detection important for maintenance and cross-platform compatibility.
AI.method: The authors developed PSASpotter, a tool that identifies the use of platform-specific APIs, while also checking for defensive code blocks like try/except or conditionals based on the platform.
AI.result: PSASpotter effectively identifies platform-specific API usage and defensive code, supporting empirical studies and providing insights for cross-platform software development.
AI.conclusion: PSASpotter aids software maintainers by flagging risky APIs and promoting better practices, making it a valuable tool for Python developers dealing with cross-platform challenges.

title: Equality, Equity, and Causality in Fairness Research: A Commentary on Cheng (2026)
comment: Invited commentary on Cheng (2026), Psychometrika, doi:10.1017/psy.2026.10110; submitted to Psychometrika
summary: This is an invited commentary on the Psychometrika focus article "Fairness Issues and Evaluation in Psychometrics and AI/ML: What Can We Learn from Each Field?" by Ying Cheng (2026, doi:10.1017/psy.2026.10110). Cheng offers a systematic comparison between long-standing test fairness and modern algorithmic fairness. Her mapping of the entire testing workflow onto the AI/ML fairness paradigm, rather than only the final selection stage, is a crucial contribution to interdisciplinary fairness research. This commentary extends her discussion by examining two conceptual issues: the distinction between equality and equity, and the role of causality in fairness research. Together, the focus article and this commentary point to directions for future fairness research across the psychometrics and AI/ML communities.

AI.tldr: This commentary explores fairness in psychometrics and AI/ML, building upon a focus article by Ying Cheng to extend discussions on equality, equity, causality, and interdisciplinary research.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to deepen understanding and foster interdisciplinary dialogue on fairness by exploring concepts of equality, equity, and causality within psychometrics and AI/ML.
AI.method: Analysis and extension of Ying Cheng's systematic comparison of fairness paradigms in psychometrics and AI/ML.
AI.result: By examining equality vs. equity and causality, the commentary refines discussions on fairness and highlights future research directions.
AI.conclusion: Collaborative research between psychometrics and AI/ML is vital to advance fairness frameworks, addressing foundational concepts for broader applications.

title: From Perception to Assistance: Open-Vocabulary Shared Autonomy for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Teleoperating a robotic manipulator in industrial environments demands precision that camera-based interfaces alone struggle to deliver. The operator must align the end-effector with a target in clutter, under limited depth perception, and without colliding with the surrounding structures. This paper presents a shared-autonomy framework that assists the operator throughout this process. A single RGB-D camera captures the operator's arm motion and hand gestures without wearables, fiducials, or a calibration stage. The intended target is specified by a free-form text prompt, grounded by a vision-language model in the robot's gripper camera, and tracked across its onboard cameras by a promptable video-segmentation model, resulting in a grasp frame continuously separated from the obstacle map. Every commanded motion is executed by a GPU-accelerated model-predictive controller that enforces self- and environment-collision avoidance against an online volumetric reconstruction, while a potential field corrects the operator's reference toward the grounded target during the final approach. An autonomous mode can be gesture-triggered to complete the grasp on the same target without a separate perception pipeline. The framework is validated on a quadruped mobile manipulator. The interface achieves a positional RMSE of 59 mm relative to motion-capture ground truth, and the controller keeps the arm at least 18 cm from obstacles while the operator deliberately commands the arm into them by 6 cm. In an industrial valve manipulation and a pick-and-place task, the full framework succeeded in all trials, while ablating either the collision or the assistance module produced failures through complementary mechanisms, and autonomous execution succeeded in four of five trials per task.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a shared-autonomy framework for teleoperating robotic manipulators in cluttered environments, using gesture-based commands and vision-language models for precision without wearables or calibration.
AI.motivation: The challenge lies in achieving precision for robotic teleoperation in cluttered industrial settings where camera-based interfaces lack depth perception and collision avoidance.
AI.method: The framework combines RGB-D camera-based hand gesture tracking, a vision-language model for target identification, a video-segmentation model for obstacle mapping, and a GPU-accelerated controller for collision avoidance. Autonomous mode can be triggered by gestures.
AI.result: The system achieves a positional RMSE of 59 mm, obstacle-maintaining distances of 18 cm, and succeeds in industrial tasks. Failures occurred when certain modules were removed or bypassed.
AI.conclusion: The shared-autonomy framework effectively enhances precision, safety, and usability in teleoperating robotic manipulators, validating its utility for industrial applications.

title: The Devil is in the Dark Pixels: Toward Brightness Bias-Robust Denoising
summary: In this paper, we reveal an important yet overlooked problem in image denoising: under signal-dependent camera noise models, dark regions suffer from inherently low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), as signal intensity decays far faster than noise variance diminishes, making detail recovery in dark areas fundamentally challenging. Yet rather than compensating for this difficulty, MSE-trained denoisers exacerbate it -- reconstructing dark pixels up to 6x worse relative to their per-band noise floor. This bias stems from two compounding factors: signal-dependent noise inflates bright-pixel residuals, and the network's Jacobian norm increases monotonically with brightness. Together, these cause bright regions to chronically dominate gradient updates at the expense of dark ones. To this end, we propose Brightness Bias-Robust Denoising (BBRD), a drop-in replacement for MSE loss that partitions pixels into brightness bands, normalizes per-band error by empirical noise variance, and applies Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (Group-DRO) to dynamically upweight whichever band is currently worst, with zero additional parameters or inference cost. Across 8 architectures and 2 datasets in our experiments, BBRD is the only method among 13 tested alternatives that improves each brightness band simultaneously, achieving up to +0.45 dB on dark bands, +0.32 dB on bright bands, and +0.65 dB aggregate Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) on SIDD, with the largest per-band gains in the darkest regions where detail recovery matters most. Code is available at https://github.com/xmed-lab/BBRD

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BBRD, a novel loss function for image denoising under signal-dependent camera noise, alleviating bias that compromises dark region quality. Four experiments demonstrate this method’s superiority across architectures and datasets.
AI.motivation: The motivation of this paper lies in addressing a critical limitation in image denoising for dark regions under signal-dependent camera noise models. These areas inherently suffer low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), yet current denoisers trained on MSE loss amplify the challenges in reconstructing dark pixels efficiently.
AI.method: The proposed method, Brightness Bias-Robust Denoising (BBRD), partitions pixels into brightness bands, normalizes errors per band based on noise variance, and employs Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (Group-DRO) to dynamically focus updates on the most error-prone band. This makes the method effective across brightness levels without adding computational overhead.
AI.result: Experiments across 8 architectures and 2 datasets reveal BBRD improves denoising performance across all brightness bands, including specific gains of up to +0.65 dB PSNR aggregate on the SIDD dataset and substantial improvements in darker regions.
AI.conclusion: BBRD presents a robust, computationally efficient approach to overcoming brightness bias in denoisers. It levels performance across brightness bands, improving critical dark region recovery without requiring extra runtime parameters.

title: Neural Controlled Differential Equations for EMT-Level Surrogate Modeling of Grid-Forming Inverters
summary: The application of artificial intelligence methods in power electronic converter modeling is becoming increasingly widespread, but existing applications still face many challenges, such as difficulties in multi-time-scale hybrid analysis and the lack of physics-aware evaluation criteria and constraints, resulting in poor performance. This paper proposes a Neural Controlled Differential Equation (Neural CDE) framework for learning continuous-time surrogate models of grid-forming inverters for electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulation, which relaxes the constraint of fixed sampling rates and enables multi-time-scale control analysis. Then, an affine-control formulation with dual slow/fast pathways is proposed to capture the hierarchical and multiscale behavior of converter dynamics, and a physics-inspired regularization method is utilized to enhance stability and coherence. Evaluated on EMT-generated trajectories, the model accurately reproduces transient responses, preserves effective damping and the dominant oscillatory characteristics, and maintains bounded long-horizon rollouts. The results show that Neural CDE-based component modeling offers a physically consistent surrogate modeling approach for EMT-level simulation studies.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Neural CDE framework for EMT simulations of grid-forming inverters, enabling multi-time-scale analysis and addressing physics-aware constraints for better model performance.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for modeling power electronic converters struggle with multi-time-scale analysis and lack physics-aware evaluation, leading to inadequate performance.
AI.method: A Neural Controlled Differential Equation (Neural CDE) framework is introduced along with an affine-control formulation and physics-inspired regularization for stability and multiscale analysis.
AI.result: The model accurately reproduces grid-forming inverter dynamics, retains damping and oscillatory characteristics, and ensures stable long-term simulations on EMT-generated data.
AI.conclusion: Neural CDE-based models provide a physically consistent, effective tool for surrogate modeling in EMT simulations of grid-forming inverters.

title: DS@GT ARC at eRisk 2026: Hybrid Multi-Agent LLM System with Structured Algorithmic Guidance for Conversational Depression Screening
comment: Conference and Labs of the Evaluation Forum (CLEF), 19 pages, 5 figures
summary: We describe DS@GT's submission to the eRisk 2026 Task 1 challenge on conversational depression screening, in which systems interview LLM personas that simulate individuals with varying depression profiles and produce a Beck Depression Inventory II (BDI-II) score plus four key symptoms per persona, without directly asking sensitive mental health questions. Our pipeline evolved through three stages: a monolithic single-model prototype to start off, a baseline multi-agent architecture that separates conversational interviewing from BDI-II scoring under a coordinating orchestration layer, and a final hybrid configuration that replaces the paid GPT-5-nano interviewer with the open-source Gemma 27B. To offset the model's weaker reasoning and instruction-following, the hybrid adds three algorithmic components: a precomputed dialogue tree that standardizes interview openers and follow-ups, a reliability-weighted consensus aggregation inspired by the Weaver framework, and a cluster-based imputation step for unprobed symptoms. We submitted three fully automated runs across all 20 personas, with Run 1 from the paid baseline and Runs 2 and 3 from the hybrid. Hybrid Run 3 achieved an ADODL of 0.9063, ranking 3rd among all complete-submission runs and placing DS@GT 2nd among the 21 teams overall, while outperforming our paid baseline Run 1 (0.8841) at roughly one-quarter of the per-persona API cost.
